Recital 11 EU requirements for product data and their physical conception*
Union law establishing physical design and data requirements for products to be placed on the Union market should not be affected unless specifically provided for by this Regulation.

example_name: Apple iCloud Family Sharing Data Access Issues
example_background: Apple's iCloud service enables family sharing where multiple users access shared content and data through a family organizer account. Users reported difficulties in managing individual data access, with family members unable to separately control or delete their personal data generated from device usage, such as photos, backups, and app data. The system relied on a primary account holder granting permissions, which led to situations where ex-family members retained access to data post-group changes, and individual account deletion did not fully erase shared data traces. This affected connected devices like iPhones and iPads that generate continuous data streams. Complaints highlighted the lack of granular controls for co-users in shared setups, requiring manual intervention by the organizer rather than automated mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: Users filed complaints with the FTC and EU data protection authorities; Apple updated its Family Sharing features in iOS 16 to include better account separation and deletion options, resolving many issues without formal fines but following regulatory scrutiny.
source_rule:
Recital 21 Data Access and User Management for Co-Users of Connected Products and Services*
Where several persons or entities are considered to be users, for example in the case of co-ownership or where an owner, renter or lessee shares rights of data access or use, the design of the connected product or related service, or the relevant interface, should enable each user to have access to the data they generate. Use of connected products that generate data typically requires a user account to be set up. Such an account allows the user to be identified by the data holder, which may be the manufacturer. It can also be used as a means of communication and to submit and process data access requests. Where several manufacturers or related services providers have sold, rented or leased connected products or provided related services, integrated together, to the same user, the user should turn to each of the parties with which it has a contract. Manufacturers or designers of a connected product that is typically used by several persons should put in place the necessary mechanisms to allow separate user accounts for individual persons, where relevant, or for the possibility of several persons using the same user account. Account solutions should allow users to delete their accounts and erase the data related to them and could allow users to terminate data access, use or sharing, or submit requests to terminate, in particular taking into account situations in which the ownership or usage of the connected product changes. Access should be granted to the user on the basis of simple request mechanism granting automatic execution and not requiring examination or clearance by the manufacturer or data holder. This means that the data should be made available only when the user actually wants access. Where automated execution of the data access request is not possible, for example via a user account or accompanying mobile application provided with the connected product or related service, the manufacturer should inform the user as to how the data may be accessed.

example_name: Google Nest Multi-User Account Problems
example_background: Google's Nest smart home devices, including thermostats and cameras, support multiple users in a household sharing access via the Google Home app. Initial designs tied data access primarily to the home owner’s account, limiting co-users' ability to independently access, manage, or delete their generated data such as activity logs and voice recordings. Shared users faced challenges in terminating access upon leaving the household, with data persisting unless the owner manually revoked permissions. This setup lacked automated deletion tied to individual accounts, affecting renters and shared living situations where devices generate personal usage data continuously.
process_and_outcome: Following user complaints and privacy audits, Google introduced multiple account support and easier access revocation in 2021 updates; no major fines but part of broader Nest privacy settlements.

example_name: Ring Doorbell Shared Access Enforcement
example_background: Amazon's Ring video doorbells allow account owners to share live view and recorded video access with family or neighbors. Prior to 2023 updates, shared users could not independently delete their access or associated data, and ownership transfers did not automatically terminate prior shares, leading to unauthorized continued access to home video feeds generated by the device. Multiple households reported issues where ex-partners or previous roommates retained data access without simple revocation mechanisms, relying on owner intervention through the app.
process_and_outcome: FTC investigated privacy practices, resulting in a $5.8 million settlement in 2023; Amazon enhanced shared access controls with automated termination options.

example_name: Tesla Full Self-Driving Data Sharing Disputes
example_background: Tesla vehicles collect extensive driving data through Full Self-Driving (FSD) features, shared among family members using the same account on connected cars. Users in shared ownership scenarios, such as spouses or car-sharing services, could not easily access or delete their individual driving data logs, cabin camera footage, or telemetry without affecting others. The single-account model for vehicles limited separate user management, and data requests required Tesla's review rather than automatic provision.
process_and_outcome: Class-action lawsuits and NHTSA inquiries prompted Tesla to improve data portability tools in 2024 software updates, allowing better per-user data export.

example_name: Samsung SmartThings Multi-User Compliance Updates
example_background: Samsung's SmartThings platform connects appliances and hubs used by multiple household members. Early versions used a primary account for all device data access, making it difficult for co-users to manage personal data like usage patterns from shared washers or TVs. Following feedback, Samsung implemented separate user profiles allowing individual data access, deletion, and sharing termination directly via the app.
process_and_outcome: Samsung rolled out multi-user support in 2022, praised in industry reviews for enabling granular controls and automatic request handling.

This means that the data should be made available only when the user actually wants access. Where automated execution of the data access request is not possible, for example via a user account or accompanying mobile application provided with the connected product or related service, the manufacturer should inform the user as to how the data may be accessed.

example_name: Philips Hue Bridge Account Management Best Practice
example_background: Philips Hue smart lighting systems use a Bridge that supports multiple user accounts for shared homes, renters, or co-owners. Each user can set up individual accounts via the app, access their generated data (usage schedules, motion data), delete it independently, and terminate access without owner approval. Ownership transfers prompt automatic data segregation prompts.
process_and_outcome: Recognized in privacy certifications and user studies for seamless multi-user support, avoiding complaints and regulatory issues.
source_rule:
Recital 21 Data Access and User Management for Co-Users of Connected Products and Services*
Where several persons or entities are considered to be users, for example in the case of co-ownership or where an owner, renter or lessee shares rights of data access or use, the design of the connected product or related service, or the relevant interface, should enable each user to have access to the data they generate. Use of connected products that generate data typically requires a user account to be set up. Such an account allows the user to be identified by the data holder, which may be the manufacturer. It can also be used as a means of communication and to submit and process data access requests. Where several manufacturers or related services providers have sold, rented or leased connected products or provided related services, integrated together, to the same user, the user should turn to each of the parties with which it has a contract. Manufacturers or designers of a connected product that is typically used by several persons should put in place the necessary mechanisms to allow separate user accounts for individual persons, where relevant, or for the possibility of several persons using the same user account. Account solutions should allow users to delete their accounts and erase the data related to them and could allow users to terminate data access, use or sharing, or submit requests to terminate, in particular taking into account situations in which the ownership or usage of the connected product changes. Access should be granted to the user on the basis of simple request mechanism granting automatic execution and not requiring examination or clearance by the manufacturer or data holder. This means that the data should be made available only when the user actually wants access. Where automated execution of the data access request is not possible, for example via a user account or accompanying mobile application provided with the connected product or related service, the manufacturer should inform the user as to how the data may be accessed.

example_name: Fitbit Family Account Data Access Settlement
example_background: Fitbit devices track health data shared in family accounts, where children or spouses used parent-managed accounts. Co-users faced barriers to accessing or deleting their personal fitness and sleep data independently, with deletion requiring parent action and no automated mechanisms for account separation upon usage changes.
process_and_outcome: Google (post-Fitbit acquisition) settled with class-action plaintiffs in 2022, agreeing to enhance account controls; paid $25 million in refunds.

example_name: Sonos Multi-Room Audio User Management
example_background: Sonos speakers in shared living spaces allow multiple user accounts linked to the same system. Users can request access to playback history and preferences data automatically via the app, delete accounts with data erasure, and terminate sharing for specific rooms or devices without central approval.
process_and_outcome: Sonos received positive feedback in privacy audits and avoided enforcement actions through proactive design.

example_name: Wyze Cam Shared User Revocation Failures
example_background: Wyze security cameras permit sharing camera feeds with multiple users. A 2020 breach exposed shared user data, and standard sharing lacked easy revocation or individual data deletion, allowing ex-users to retain access to motion detection videos unless manually unshared by the owner.
process_and_outcome: Wyze faced FTC scrutiny and class-action suits, leading to $2.4 million settlement and improved sharing controls in 2021.

example_name: Amazon Alexa Data Access Dispute with California AG
example_background: Amazon's Alexa virtual assistant collects extensive data from user interactions with connected products in smart homes, such as lights, thermostats, and security cameras from various manufacturers. The device records voice commands, usage patterns, and environmental data processed through Alexa as the central gateway, replacing direct manufacturer apps. Users sought access to this interaction data generated exclusively via Alexa with third-party devices, but Amazon's policies limited sharing to aggregated or anonymized forms, restricting portability to device makers like Philips Hue or Ecobee for service improvements. This arose amid broader concerns over smart home data silos where virtual assistants control access to product telemetry.
process_and_outcome: California Attorney General investigated under consumer protection laws, leading to a 2023 settlement where Amazon agreed to enhance data access tools, delete certain stored recordings, and pay $25 million. Additional audits ensured compliance with data portability requests for interaction logs.
source_rule:
Recital 23 Data Access for Virtual Assistents and Connected Products*
Virtual assistants play an increasing role in digitising consumer and professional environments and serve as an easy-to-use interface to play content, obtain information, or activate products connected to the internet. Virtual assistants can act as a single gateway in, for example, a smart home environment and record significant amounts of relevant data on how users interact with products connected to the internet, including those manufactured by other parties, and can replace the use of manufacturer-provided interfaces such as touch screens or smartphone apps. The user may wish to make available such data to third party manufacturers and enable novel smart services. Virtual assistants should be covered by the data access rights provided for in this Regulation. Data generated when a user interacts with a connected product via a virtual assistant provided by an entity other than the manufacturer of the connected product should also be covered by the data access rights provided for in this Regulation. However, only the data arising from the interaction between the user and a connected product or related service through the virtual assistant should be covered by this Regulation. Data produced by the virtual assistant which are unrelated to the use of a connected product or related service are not covered by this Regulation.

example_name: Google Nest Hub Data Portability Enforcement by CNIL
example_background: Google's Nest Hub serves as a virtual assistant hub for smart homes, aggregating data from connected products like thermostats, bulbs, and locks from multiple vendors including Nest, Philips, and TP-Link. It captures user commands, device states, and interaction histories through voice and gesture interfaces, acting as the primary access point over manufacturer-specific apps. Users and third-party manufacturers requested raw interaction data to develop competing services, but Google provided only limited exports excluding full product-specific telemetry generated via Nest.
process_and_outcome: French CNIL (data protection authority) conducted a 2022 inquiry, fining Google €150 million for data access restrictions. Google updated its APIs to allow user-initiated data transfers to third parties for connected product interactions.

example_name: Apple HomeKit Data Sharing Framework
example_background: Apple's HomeKit platform integrates Siri as a virtual assistant for connected products from third-party makers like Eve, Nanoleaf, and Ecobee. It enables users to control smart home devices via Siri, generating data on interactions such as automation triggers, sensor readings, and command histories. Apple designed the system to allow users to export and share this specific interaction data with original manufacturers or third services upon request, distinguishing it from pure Siri-generated data unrelated to devices.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; Apple's implementation received praise in EU consultations for the Data Act, serving as a model for compliance. Users successfully ported data to improve device firmware via HomeKit Accessory Protocol.

example_name: Samsung SmartThings Data Access Settlement with FTC
example_background: Samsung's SmartThings hub uses Bixby virtual assistant to interface with thousands of connected products from other brands, collecting data on user interactions like lighting schedules, lock events, and appliance usage. As a gateway replacing individual device apps, it amassed detailed logs. Complaints emerged when users and manufacturers could not access raw interaction data for analytics or service enhancements, with Samsung offering only dashboard views.
process_and_outcome: FTC investigated in 2021, resulting in a consent order requiring Samsung to implement data portability APIs and delete geofence data improperly retained. Fine of $4.5 million and ongoing compliance monitoring followed.

example_name: Matter Standard Implementation by Amazon and Google
example_background: The Matter 1.0 standard, launched in 2022, enables interoperable data access across virtual assistants like Alexa and Google Assistant for connected products. It mandates that interaction data generated through these assistants—such as motion sensor activations or thermostat adjustments from diverse manufacturers—be accessible to users and original equipment makers (OEMs), bypassing silos and supporting user-directed sharing for new services.
process_and_outcome: Connectivity Standards Alliance certified implementations, with Amazon and Google rolling out updates in 2023. This preempted regulatory scrutiny and was highlighted in Data Act preparatory works as aligned practice.

example_name: Sonos Data Portability Feature for Voice Assistants
example_background: Sonos speakers integrate Alexa, Google Assistant, and Sonos Voice Control as virtual assistants, handling interactions with connected home products like lights and HVAC systems. Data from these sessions, including multi-device commands and usage patterns, is generated via the speaker as the interface. Sonos introduced user controls in 2023 to export and share this data with product manufacturers, excluding unrelated assistant outputs.
process_and_outcome: Voluntary rollout praised by privacy advocates; no fines or actions. Used in industry pilots for Data Act compliance testing.

example_name: Ring Amazon Data Access Investigation by ICO
example_background: Ring devices, owned by Amazon, use Alexa for virtual assistant control of doorbells and cameras, interfacing with smart home ecosystems. Interactions generate data like motion events and sharing requests with third-party locks or lights. UK users requested access to interaction logs for manufacturer integrations, but Ring provided incomplete datasets tied to its app.
process_and_outcome: UK ICO fined Ring £5.4 million in 2021 for privacy lapses including data access denials. Remediation included API expansions for user data exports.

Microsoft enabled data export features for user-requested sharing with OEMs, focusing on product-related interactions.
process_and_outcome: Compliance audit by Irish DPC in 2019 found adherence; no fines. Practices informed later Invoke speaker data handling.

example_name: Facebook Portal Data Access Fine by Italian Garante
example_background: Facebook (Meta) Portal devices featured voice assistants interfacing with smart home products, recording interaction data like commands to Philips Hue lights or Nest cameras. process_and_outcome: Italian Garante fined Meta €12 million in 2020, mandating improved data access for interaction logs and user portability options.

example_name: John Deere Right to Repair Data Access Disputes
example_background: John Deere, a leading manufacturer of agricultural machinery, produces connected tractors and equipment that generate extensive data on farm operations, crop yields, and machinery performance. Farmers purchasing or leasing these products have limited options for accessing this data due to proprietary software and diagnostic tools controlled exclusively by the company. Independent repair shops and third-party developers face barriers in obtaining necessary software updates or interfaces, restricting their ability to service equipment or develop data-driven applications. This situation limits data sharing with agritech startups and affects farmers' ability to derive value from the data produced by their investments. Advocacy groups highlighted how this concentration of control by a few manufacturers hampers innovation and competition in the data economy surrounding precision agriculture.
process_and_outcome: US lawmakers introduced the Right to Repair Act in response, and John Deere faced public pressure leading to announcements of expanded software access programs for owners. No formal fines were imposed, but ongoing FTC inquiries and state-level legislation pushed for better data access tools.
source_rule:
Recital 27 Legislation Authorisation to address sector-specific needs and objectives*
In sectors characterised by the concentration of a small number of manufacturers supplying connected products to end users, there may only be limited options available to users for the access to and the use and sharing of data. In such circumstances, contracts may be insufficient to achieve the objective of user empowerment, making it difficult for users to obtain value from the data generated by the connected product they purchase, rent or lease. Consequently, there is limited potential for innovative smaller businesses to offer data-based solutions in a competitive manner and for a diverse data economy in the Union. This Regulation should therefore build on recent developments in specific sectors, such as the Code of Conduct on agricultural data sharing by contract. Union or national law may be adopted to address sector-specific needs and objectives. Furthermore, data holders should not use any readily available data that is non-personal data in order to derive insights about the economic situation of the user or its assets or production methods or about such use by the user in any other manner that could undermine the commercial position of that user on the markets in which it is active. This could include using knowledge about the overall performance of a business or a farm in contractual negotiations with the user on the potential acquisition of the user’s products or agricultural produce to the user’s detriment, or using such information to feed into larger databases on certain markets in the aggregate, for example databases on crop yields for the upcoming harvesting season, as such use could affect the user negatively in an indirect manner. The user should be given the necessary technical interface to manage permissions, preferably with granular permission options such as ‘allow once’ or ‘allow while using this app or service’, including the option to withdraw such permissions.
involved_parties: Farmers and repair advocacy groups like Repair.org
relevant_rules: Data Act Recital 27 principles (analogous EU framework)
Agricultural Right to Repair Act proposals

example_name: EU Data Act Agricultural Code of Conduct Implementation
example_background: The EU-supported Code of Conduct on agricultural data sharing was developed by industry stakeholders including manufacturers like CNH Industrial and CLAAS, crop protection firms like BASF, and farmer organizations. It addresses sectors with few dominant suppliers of connected equipment where users have limited data access options. The code establishes voluntary standards for data portability, permission management, and sharing generated data with third parties upon user request, building on the recognition that contracts alone are insufficient for user empowerment in concentrated markets.
process_and_outcome: Adopted in 2018 and updated, the code has been implemented by signatories, leading to standardized data formats and access tools without regulatory enforcement actions. It serves as a model referenced in the Data Act.

example_name: Amazon Sidewalk Data Sharing Controversy
example_background: Amazon's Sidewalk network, involving connected IoT devices like Echo speakers and Ring cameras from a dominant manufacturer, shares device-generated data across users' networks to extend coverage. Users have limited control over data access and sharing, with opt-out options buried in settings rather than granular permissions like 'allow once'. This setup in a market with few alternatives raises concerns about data holders deriving insights on user assets and usage patterns without clear empowerment tools.
process_and_outcome: Consumer complaints led to FTC scrutiny and class-action lawsuits alleging unauthorized data sharing. Amazon adjusted privacy settings in 2021, but investigations continued into data access practices.

example_name: Tesla Vehicle Data Access for Third Parties
example_background: Tesla vehicles generate vast telemetry data on performance, location, and usage, controlled by the company in a market with limited alternatives for electric vehicles with similar connectivity. Owners and fleet operators reported difficulties accessing raw data for insurance, maintenance, or third-party apps, relying on Tesla's premium connectivity services. This limits smaller businesses from offering data-based services like predictive maintenance or efficiency analytics.
process_and_outcome: European regulators under investigation for Data Act compliance; Tesla expanded API access in 2023 via developer programs, but granular user permission tools remain limited.

This Regulation should therefore build on recent developments in specific sectors, such as the Code of Conduct on agricultural data sharing by contract. Furthermore, data holders should not use any readily available data that is non-personal data in order to derive insights about the economic situation of the user or its assets or production methods or about such use by the user in any other manner that could undermine the commercial position of that user on the markets in which it is active. This could include using knowledge about the overall performance of a business or a farm in contractual negotiations with the user on the potential acquisition of the user’s products or agricultural produce to the user’s detriment, or using such information to feed into larger databases on certain markets in the aggregate, for example databases on crop yields for the upcoming harvesting season, as such use could affect the user negatively in an indirect manner. The user should be given the necessary technical interface to manage permissions, preferably with granular permission options such as ‘allow once’ or ‘allow while using this app or service’, including the option to withdraw such permissions.

example_name: Apple Health Data Permission Controls
example_background: Apple's Health app and connected wearables collect extensive user data on fitness, biometrics, and activity from devices in a concentrated market. The company provides granular permission options including 'allow once', time-limited access, and easy withdrawal via iOS settings, enabling users to control data sharing with third-party apps and services while preventing data holders from deriving unpermitted insights.
process_and_outcome: Praised in privacy reports; no enforcement actions, positioned as industry best practice influencing regulations.
source_rule:

example_name: Fitbit Google Acquisition Data Access Scrutiny
example_background: Following Google's 2021 acquisition of Fitbit, concerns arose over access to health and fitness data generated by wearables in a market dominated by few players. Users faced uncertainties about data portability and controls, with reports of limited options for exporting data or managing third-party access, potentially allowing the data holder to derive insights on user health patterns.
process_and_outcome: EU Commission approved merger with commitments for third-party access to Fitbit data APIs for 10 years; ongoing monitoring.

example_name: CNH Industrial Farm Data Sharing Portal
example_background: CNH Industrial, maker of connected farm equipment like New Holland tractors, launched a data sharing platform allowing farmers to access, manage, and share telemetry data with agronomists and service providers. In a sector with few suppliers, this provides technical interfaces for permissions, addressing limitations in contractual data access.
process_and_outcome: Implemented as part of EU Code of Conduct; recognized as best practice with no violations recorded.

example_name: Caterpillar Cat Connect Data Access Issues
example_background: Caterpillar's Cat Connect telematics on mining and construction equipment generate data on asset performance and operations. Customers in concentrated industrial sectors report restricted access to raw data, limiting third-party analytics firms and requiring proprietary subscriptions, which hinders data economy development.
process_and_outcome: Customer lawsuits and antitrust complaints in the US; Caterpillar expanded data export features in 2022.

example_name: John Deere Operations Center Data Hub Compliance
example_background: John Deere's Operations Center platform evolved to offer farmers a dashboard for managing permissions on data from connected machinery, including sharing with partners. This responds to sector-specific needs for data access in agriculture, providing tools for granular control amid limited supplier options.
process_and_outcome: Launched updates in 2021 aligning with right-to-repair and data sharing codes; positive industry feedback.

example_name: Apple iCloud Private Relay Data Access Issues
example_background: Apple introduced iCloud Private Relay as part of iCloud+ service in iOS 15, designed to route internet traffic through two separate relays to protect user privacy. The service processes user data via Apple's servers and a third-party partner, obscuring IP addresses from websites and Apple itself. Concerns arose when data generated by connected Apple devices, including location and browsing metadata, was stored and accessed through remote servers without fully transparent user controls for direct on-device access or third-party processing options. This setup involved processors handling data relay functions under Apple's direction as controller, raising questions about data accessibility in connected ecosystems like iPhones and Macs. The design allowed data communication to servers but limited direct user extraction from device storage without iCloud subscription dependencies.
process_and_outcome: EU regulators and privacy advocates scrutinized the service under GDPR for data minimization and purpose limitation. Apple updated privacy labels and provided more details on data flows in 2022. No formal fines were imposed, but ongoing monitoring by Irish DPC ensured compliance adjustments, including enhanced user notices on data server access.
source_rule:
Recital 22 Design of Data Access and Use of Processor*
Connected products may be designed to make certain data directly accessible from on-device data storage or from a remote server to which the data are communicated. Access to on-device data storage may be enabled via cable-based or wireless local area networks connected to a publicly available electronic communications service or mobile network. The server may be the manufacturer’s own local server capacity or that of a third party or a cloud service provider. Processors as defined in Article 4, point (8), of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 are not considered to act as data holders. However, they can be specifically tasked with making data available by the controller as defined in Article 4, point (7), of Regulation (EU) 2016/679. Connected products may be designed to permit the user or a third party to process the data on the connected product, on a computing instance of the manufacturer or within an information and communications technology (ICT) environment chosen by the user or the third party.

example_name: Google Nest Thermostat Data Processor Enforcement
example_background: Google's Nest thermostats collect environmental and usage data from connected devices, storing it on-device initially before transmission to Google's cloud servers for analytics and user app access. The devices use third-party cloud processors under Google's control as data controller to process temperature, humidity, and energy usage data. Users faced limitations in directly accessing raw data logs from device storage without routing through Google's servers or apps, and third-party processing was restricted to Google's ecosystem. This design impacted data portability when users switched to non-Google smart home systems, as data was not easily extractable via local networks or cables.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, NOYB filed complaints with EU data protection authorities alleging unlawful data tying and processor dependencies. CNIL (France) and other DPAs investigated, leading Google to commit to API improvements for better third-party access by mid-2024 without fines at that stage.

example_name: Amazon Ring Doorbell Data Access GDPR Fine
example_background: Amazon's Ring video doorbells generate video and audio data stored on-device temporarily before upload to AWS cloud servers. The design enables access via Amazon's app over wireless networks but restricts direct on-device data extraction to authorized processors under Amazon's control. Employees and contractors accessed user videos without clear consent mechanisms, and third-party sharing was limited to law enforcement portals. This setup treated AWS as a processor tasked with data availability, but lacked provisions for user-chosen ICT environments or local server access, affecting millions of EU users' connected doorbells.
process_and_outcome: Dutch DPA fined Amazon €30.8 million in 2023 for transparency failures and excessive data access by processors. Amazon appealed but implemented redesigned consent tools and local storage options in firmware updates.

example_name: Fitbit Data Processor Acquisition Scrutiny
example_background: Google's 2021 acquisition of Fitbit raised concerns over handling of health data from wearables that store activity, heart rate, and sleep data on-device and sync to Fitbit/Google cloud servers via mobile networks. Processors manage data processing under Google's controller role, but initial designs did not prioritize direct user access to on-device storage or permissions for third-party cloud environments. EU commitments were made to keep Fitbit health data separate from ad profiling, yet access remained server-mediated, limiting local data use.
process_and_outcome: European Commission approved the acquisition with behavioral remedies in 2022, requiring Google to maintain fair access APIs for third parties. Ongoing audits by trustees ensure processor compliance; no fines issued post-merger.

example_name: Philips Hue Smart Lighting Data Compliance
example_background: Signify's Philips Hue bulbs and bridges store usage and configuration data on the local bridge device, accessible via Zigbee local network or the Philips cloud. The design allows users to process data on their own ICT environments through open APIs and local hub access without mandatory cloud reliance. Processors like AWS handle optional cloud syncing under Signify's control, but emphasize user-direct access from on-device storage via apps or third-party integrations like Home Assistant.
process_and_outcome: Signify participated in EU DPIA assessments voluntarily, receiving positive feedback from DPAs for data minimization. No enforcement actions; certified under Matter standard for interoperability in 2023.

example_name: Sonos Speaker Local Processing Design
example_background: Sonos wireless speakers process audio data locally on-device for features like Trueplay tuning, with optional cloud sync to Sonos servers via Wi-Fi. Users can access sound calibration data directly from device storage through developer APIs, and third parties integrate via local network access without mandatory processors. The architecture separates controller functions (Sonos Inc.) from optional cloud processors, enabling user-chosen environments.
process_and_outcome: Sonos published open-source local control protocols in 2023 after community feedback, aligning with data access principles. No regulatory actions; praised in industry reports for user-centric design.

example_name: Bosch Smart Home Gateway Data Access
example_background: Bosch's Smart Home Gateway acts as an on-device hub for connected sensors and appliances, storing data locally accessible via Ethernet or Wi-Fi. Users and third parties can process data within chosen ICT setups using open standards like Matter and OCF. Bosch tasks processors only for optional remote features, maintaining controller oversight while prioritizing direct storage access.
process_and_outcome: Certified compliant in EU audits 2022; collaborated with ENX Association for cybersecurity. No fines or investigations reported.

example_name: Samsung SmartThings Hub Processor Transparency
example_background: Samsung's SmartThings platform uses hubs that store device data on-device, with access enabled via local networks or cloud. Following GDPR scrutiny, Samsung enhanced designs for user-direct data extraction and third-party processing permissions, distinguishing controller (Samsung) from cloud processors.
process_and_outcome: UK ICO closed investigation in 2021 with no action after Samsung's remediation, including local API expansions.

example_name: Meta Quest VR Headset Data Flow Violations
example_background: Meta's Quest VR headsets capture eye-tracking, motion, and usage data stored on-device before upload to Meta's servers via Wi-Fi. Processors handle data under Meta's control, but designs limited direct user access to raw logs, funneling all processing through Meta's ecosystem and restricting third-party local use.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion in 2023 primarily for transfers, with secondary notes on processing design; Meta redesigned VR privacy dashboards.

example_name: Fitbit Data Sharing with Third Parties
example_background: Fitbit, a wearable fitness tracker manufacturer, collected extensive non-personal data from users' devices, including heart rate, sleep patterns, and activity metrics generated by connected products. Terms of service allowed Fitbit to aggregate this data and share derived datasets with third parties for purposes such as research and product development. Users reported lack of clear transparency in privacy policies regarding how raw product data was processed into anonymized sets and shared, leading to concerns over consent for data use beyond basic service provision. The data was part of connected product ecosystems where users purchased devices expecting data to remain primarily for personal use.
process_and_outcome: In 2019, class action lawsuits in the U.S. alleged inadequate disclosure of data practices. Fitbit settled for $13 million without admitting wrongdoing. Google completed acquisition of Fitbit in 2021, prompting FTC review of data handling commitments. No major regulatory fines under EU data laws at the time, but it influenced scrutiny in Data Act deliberations.
source_rule:
Recital 25 Principle of contractual basis for data use*
This Regulation should not be understood to confer any new right on data holders to use product data or related service data. Where the manufacturer of a connected product is a data holder, the basis for the manufacturer to use non-personal data should be a contract between the manufacturer and the user. Such a contract could be part of an agreement for the provision of the related service, which could be concluded together with the purchase, rent or lease agreement relating to the connected product. Any contractual term stipulating that the data holder may use product data or related service data should be transparent to the user, including regarding the purposes for which the data holder intends to use the data. Such purposes could include improving the functioning of the connected product or related services, developing new products or services, or aggregating data with the aim of making available the resulting derived data to third parties, provided that such derived data do not allow the identification of specific data transmitted to the data holder from the connected product, or allow a third party to derive those data from the dataset. Any change of the contract should depend on the informed agreement of the user. This Regulation does not prevent parties from agreeing on contractual terms the effect of which is to exclude or limit the use of non-personal data, or certain categories of non-personal data, by a data holder. Neither does it prevent parties from agreeing to make product data or related service data available to third parties, directly or indirectly, including, where applicable, via another data holder. Moreover, this Regulation does not prevent sector-specific regulatory requirements under Union law, or national law compatible with Union law, which would exclude or limit the use of certain such data by the data holder on well-defined public policy grounds. This Regulation does not prevent users, in the case of business-to-business relations, from making data available to third parties or data holders under any lawful contractual term, including by agreeing to limit or restrict further sharing of such data, or from being compensated proportionately, for example in exchange for waiving their right to use or share such data. While the notion of ‘data holder’ generally does not include public sector bodies, it may include public undertakings.

example_name: John Deere Precision Agriculture Data Policy
example_background: John Deere's connected tractors and farm equipment generate non-personal data on crop yields, soil conditions, and machinery performance through Precision Ag technologies. The company's Data Share agreement requires farmers to contractually agree to data use for improving equipment, developing services, and sharing aggregated data with third parties like seed companies, provided it does not identify specific farm data. Terms are presented during equipment purchase or software activation, with options to limit sharing, and changes require user notification.
process_and_outcome: Introduced in 2020, the policy resolved farmer complaints about unauthorized data access. No regulatory enforcement; adopted as industry standard, with USDA referencing it positively in digital ag reports.

example_name: Google Nest Thermostat Data Practices Fine
example_background: Google's Nest smart thermostats collect energy usage, temperature, and occupancy data from connected home devices. In 2022, the French data protection authority found Google failed to provide transparent information on data retention periods and purposes for using non-personal device data, including aggregation for energy efficiency services and sharing with partners. Contracts bundled with device purchase did not clearly separate data use consents.
process_and_outcome: CNIL fined Google 150 million euros, the largest GDPR fine to date, mandating policy updates and audits. Google appealed but implemented changes.

Neither does it prevent parties from agreeing to make product data or related service data available to third parties, directly or indirectly, including, where applicable, via another data holder. Moreover, this Regulation does not prevent sector-specific regulatory requirements under Union law, or national law compatible with Union law, which would exclude or limit the use of certain such data by the data holder on well-defined public policy grounds. This Regulation does not prevent users, in the case of business-to-business relations, from making data available to third parties or data holders under any lawful contractual term, including by agreeing to limit or restrict further sharing of such data, or from being compensated proportionately, for example in exchange for waiving their right to use or share such data. While the notion of ‘data holder’ generally does not include public sector bodies, it may include public undertakings.

example_name: Microsoft Xbox Data Compliance Practices
example_background: Microsoft's Xbox consoles generate gameplay, usage, and telemetry data from connected gaming products. The Xbox privacy policy requires explicit user agreements for data use in service improvements, anti-cheat systems, and aggregated analytics shared with developers. Changes to terms trigger notifications and opt-in prompts, with options for users to restrict data sharing in B2C and B2B contexts.
process_and_outcome: During 2023 CMA merger review with Activision, regulators confirmed compliance with data transparency. No fines; recognized in EU reports as model for gaming sector.
source_rule:
Recital 25 Principle of contractual basis for data use*
This Regulation should not be understood to confer any new right on data holders to use product data or related service data. Where the manufacturer of a connected product is a data holder, the basis for the manufacturer to use non-personal data should be a contract between the manufacturer and the user. Such a contract could be part of an agreement for the provision of the related service, which could be concluded together with the purchase, rent or lease agreement relating to the connected product. Any contractual term stipulating that the data holder may use product data or related service data should be transparent to the user, including regarding the purposes for which the data holder intends to use the data. Such purposes could include improving the functioning of the connected product or related services, developing new products or services, or aggregating data with the aim of making available the resulting derived data to third parties, provided that such derived data do not allow the identification of specific data transmitted to the data holder from the connected product, or allow a third party to derive those data from the dataset. Any change of the contract should depend on the informed agreement of the user. This Regulation does not prevent parties from agreeing on contractual terms the effect of which is to exclude or limit the use of non-personal data, or certain categories of non-personal data, by a data holder. Neither does it prevent parties from agreeing to make product data or related service data available to third parties, directly or indirectly, including, where applicable, via another data holder. Moreover, this Regulation does not prevent sector-specific regulatory requirements under Union law, or national law compatible with Union law, which would exclude or limit the use of certain such data by the data holder on well-defined public policy grounds. This Regulation does not prevent users, in the case of business-to-business relations, from making data available to third parties or data holders under any lawful contractual term, including by agreeing to limit or restrict further sharing of such data, or from being compensated proportionately, for example in exchange for waiving their right to use or share such data. While the notion of ‘data holder’ generally does not include public sector bodies, it may include public undertakings.

example_name: Tesla Autopilot Data Usage Controversy
example_background: Tesla vehicles with Autopilot generate vast non-personal data on driving patterns, camera feeds, and sensor inputs from connected cars. Tesla's privacy notice allows use for improving Full Self-Driving software and sharing aggregated data with regulators or partners, but opt-in for data sharing was not always clearly presented at purchase. Firmware updates changed data practices without explicit user re-consent, leading to NHTSA inquiries into data handling.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA investigated crashes involving Autopilot, expanding to data practices in 2021. Tesla enhanced disclosures; no fine issued but ongoing monitoring required.

involved_parties: U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA)

example_name: Philips Hue Smart Lighting Data Settlement
example_background: Philips Hue connected bulbs and bridges collect usage data on lighting patterns and routines. A 2022 class action alleged Signify (Philips Lighting) shared device data with advertisers without transparent contractual terms in the purchase agreement or app setup, lacking detail on aggregation and third-party derived data purposes.
process_and_outcome: Signify settled for $8 million in the U.S., agreeing to revise privacy policies and add consent mechanisms without admitting liability.

example_name: John Deere Data Sharing Controversy
example_background: John Deere, a leading manufacturer of agricultural equipment, generates extensive non-personal data through connected tractors and farm machinery, including telemetry data on crop yields, soil conditions, and equipment performance. Farmers who purchase the equipment hold the data generated during use but face technical and contractual restrictions preventing access or sharing of this data with third parties, such as data analytics firms or marketplaces. John Deere's service agreements limit data extraction to authorized dealers, requiring farmers to pay additional fees for data access or rely on proprietary software. This setup emerged in the context of IoT-enabled farming where data liquidity could enable new markets for agronomic insights, but manufacturers retained control over data portability. Reports from 2021-2023 highlighted farmers' inability to share aggregated, non-personal datasets commercially without breaching terms of service, mirroring challenges in preparing datasets for data intermediation services due to technical lock-in and unclear rights allocation.
process_and_outcome: Public backlash and congressional hearings in the US led to advocacy for right-to-repair laws. John Deere announced expanded data access programs in 2023, allowing farmers to share operational data via APIs with third parties under certain conditions, though full contractual portability remains limited. No formal EU enforcement occurred as the Data Act was not yet applicable.
source_rule:
Recital 26 Right to commercialise non-personal data*
To foster the emergence of liquid, fair and efficient markets for non-personal data, users of connected products should be able to share data with others, including for commercial purposes, with minimal legal and technical effort. It is currently often difficult for businesses to justify the personnel or computing costs that are necessary for preparing non-personal datasets or data products and to offer them to potential counterparties via data intermediation services, including data marketplaces. A substantial hurdle to the sharing of non-personal data by businesses therefore results from the lack of predictability of economic returns from investing in the curation and making available of datasets or data products. In order to allow for the emergence of liquid, fair and efficient markets for non-personal data in the Union, the party that has the right to offer such data on a market must be clarified. Users should therefore have the right to share non-personal data with data recipients for commercial and non-commercial purposes. Such data sharing could be performed directly by the user, upon the request of the user via a data holder, or through data intermediation services. Data intermediation services, as regulated by Regulation (EU) 2022/868 of the European Parliament and of the Council could facilitate a data economy by establishing commercial relationships between users, data recipients and third parties and may support users in exercising their right to use data, such as ensuring the anonymisation of personal data or aggregation of access to data from multiple individual users. Where data are excluded from a data holder’s obligation to make them available to users or third parties, the scope of such data could be specified in the contract between the user and the data holder for the provision of a related service so that users can easily determine which data are available to them for sharing with data recipients or third parties. Data holders should not make available non-personal product data to third parties for commercial or non-commercial purposes other than the fulfilment of their contract with the user, without prejudice to legal requirements pursuant to Union or national law for a data holder to make data available. Where relevant, data holders should contractually bind third parties not to further share data received from them.
involved_parties: US farmers and advocacy groups like Farm局

example_name: Caterpillar Machine Data Access Dispute
example_background: Caterpillar Inc., a global producer of construction and mining equipment, collects vast amounts of non-personal operational data from connected machinery, such as engine hours, fuel efficiency, location telematics (non-personalized), and maintenance logs. Customers operating the equipment are users generating this data but are contractually bound to Caterpillar's VisionLink platform for access, with prohibitions on extracting or sharing raw datasets with competitors or data marketplaces without permission. This restricts the development of secondary markets for equipment performance data, as users must invest in Caterpillar's ecosystem for data processing, facing high costs and technical barriers to anonymization or aggregation for commercial purposes. The issue gained attention in 2022 amid EU discussions on data markets, where equipment data could fuel efficient marketplaces but was siloed by manufacturers.
process_and_outcome: Industry pressure and EU consultations prompted Caterpillar to introduce limited data portability features in 2023, enabling export of aggregated reports but not full raw data sharing. No fines imposed; ongoing monitoring by trade associations.

example_name: Siemens Industrial IoT Data Portability Initiative
example_background: Siemens AG developed the MindSphere platform for industrial IoT, allowing users of connected manufacturing equipment to access and share non-personal data like production metrics, machine utilization, and energy consumption with third parties. From 2018, Siemens enabled data export via open APIs, supporting integration with data marketplaces and ensuring users could commercialize aggregated datasets without technical hurdles. Contracts clearly defined data rights, excluding only proprietary algorithms, and included clauses binding recipients not to further share without permission. This facilitated liquid markets for industrial data, aligning with emerging EU frameworks by reducing preparation costs for data products.
process_and_outcome: Siemens received recognition in EU pilot projects for data spaces; no enforcement actions. The approach supported over 100 data-sharing partnerships by 2023.

example_name: GE Predix Platform Data Sharing Model
example_background: General Electric's Predix platform for industrial IoT collects non-personal data from turbines, locomotives, and healthcare equipment, including performance analytics and predictive maintenance datasets. GE structured user agreements to grant customers full rights to export and commercialize this data via standardized formats and APIs starting in 2017, facilitating sharing through data intermediation services. Users could request data from GE as data holder, with provisions for anonymization and aggregation at minimal cost. Contracts specified excludable data scopes upfront, preventing unauthorized third-party dissemination by GE.
process_and_outcome: Platform enabled commercial data marketplaces; GE exited consumer-facing Predix in 2020 but retained B2B model. Praised in industry reports for data economy contributions.

example_name: Tesla Vehicle Data Access for Fleet Operators
example_background: Tesla collects non-personal fleet data from connected vehicles, such as energy efficiency, driving patterns (aggregated), and charging station usage. Commercial fleet operators faced initial restrictions on accessing full datasets for sharing with analytics providers or marketplaces due to Tesla's cloud-based control. By 2022, Tesla expanded API access for fleet managers, allowing export of anonymized, non-personal data for commercial purposes, addressing user rights to share via direct or intermediated channels. This evolved amid regulatory scrutiny on connected car data markets.
process_and_outcome: Updates followed EU and US fleet operator complaints; Tesla integrated with data hubs like Catena-X. No penalties issued.

example_name: Komatsu Mining Equipment Data Lock-in Case
example_background: Komatsu, a heavy machinery manufacturer, uses its Komtrax system to gather non-personal data on equipment health, productivity, and site operations from connected mining vehicles. Mining companies as users encountered contractual terms prohibiting data transfer outside Komatsu's ecosystem, limiting commercialization of datasets on marketplaces. Technical barriers required proprietary software for data preparation, increasing costs and hindering fair markets for operational insights shared among operators or with AI firms.
process_and_outcome: 2023 industry lawsuits in Australia led to settlement with improved data export options. Komatsu committed to API enhancements without admitting fault.

example_name: Bosch IoT Suite Data Marketplace Integration
example_background: Bosch's IoT Suite enables users of connected devices in manufacturing and mobility to access non-personal data like sensor readings and process optimizations. Since 2019, Bosch provided tools for data anonymization, aggregation, and direct sharing with data recipients or via platforms like FIWARE, with contracts clarifying user rights and binding third parties contractually. This minimized legal and technical efforts for commercial data products, fostering efficient EU data markets.
process_and_outcome: Participated in EU Catena-X automotive data space; recognized as best practice in 2023 reports.

example_name: ABB Ability Platform User Data Rights
example_background: ABB's Ability platform for industrial automation collects non-personal data from robots and energy systems, including uptime metrics and efficiency data. ABB designed contracts to allow users full portability, supporting sharing through data intermediation services with low-effort APIs and specified exclusion scopes. This enabled commercial relationships in data marketplaces without data holder overreach.
process_and_outcome: Integrated into EU industrial data spaces; no compliance issues reported.

example_name: Volvo Construction Equipment Data Restrictions
example_background: Volvo CE's CareTrack telematics system gathers non-personal data on machine location, fuel use, and productivity from construction equipment. Operators reported difficulties in accessing raw data for sharing with third-party optimizers or marketplaces due to Volvo's terms limiting exports to dashboards and requiring fees for custom extracts, impeding data liquidity.
process_and_outcome: 2022 EU stakeholder feedback prompted API expansions in 2024. Ongoing industry dialogue.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Data Access Fines
example_background: Meta Platforms, operating Facebook and Instagram, faced multiple complaints regarding its handling of user data access requests. Users submitted DSARs to retrieve their personal data, including ad targeting profiles and behavioral data processed by numerous third-party processors for analytics and advertising. Meta required identification but often failed to verify entitlements properly, provided incomplete datasets, or delayed responses beyond the one-month deadline. In one coordinated action by NOYB across 10 EU countries, over 500 complaints highlighted that Meta did not ensure processors like measurement partners handled forwarded access requests adequately, leading to fragmented data provision.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Irish DPC fined Meta €1.2 billion for other violations but separate proceedings on DSARs led to €390 million in fines from multiple DPAs in 2022. Meta was ordered to overhaul its DSAR processes, including better processor coordination and identity verification protocols.
source_rule:
Recital 29 User Identification*
Data holders may require appropriate user identification to verify a user’s entitlement to access the data. In the case of personal data processed by a processor on behalf of the controller, data holders should ensure that the access request is received and handled by the processor.

example_name: British Airways GDPR Access Request Fine
example_background: British Airways encountered a data breach in 2018 where payment card details of 400,000 customers were accessed via a supply chain attack on a third-party processor (Magecart). Subsequently, affected users submitted data access requests to understand what personal data was compromised. BA, as controller, relied on the processor for handling parts of the request but did not ensure proper verification of user entitlements or full data disclosure. Responses were delayed, and identity checks were inconsistent, with some requests rejected without adequate explanation.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) fined British Airways £20 million in 2020 (reduced from £183 million on appeal). The decision emphasized failures in access request handling and processor coordination, mandating process improvements.
source_rule:
Recital 29 User Identification*
Data holders may require appropriate user identification to verify a user’s entitlement to access the data. In the case of personal data processed by a processor on behalf of the controller, data holders should ensure that the access request is received and handled by the processor.

example_name: Salesforce EU Data Act Readiness Program
example_background: Salesforce, a cloud-based CRM provider acting as a data processor for numerous controllers under the EU Data Act, implemented a comprehensive user identification framework ahead of the regulation's 2025 enforcement. The program includes automated verification tools for data access requests, ensuring processors confirm user entitlements before granting access to personal data held in multi-tenant environments. Salesforce coordinates with controllers to route requests properly and provides dashboards for tracking compliance, demonstrating proactive adherence to Recital 29 requirements.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; Salesforce published its compliance roadmap in 2024, earning recognition from EU regulators during Data Act consultations. Customers report 95% DSAR fulfillment within timelines.
source_rule:
Recital 29 User Identification*

example_name: Google Cloud GDPR Processor Compliance Model
example_background: Google Cloud Platform (GCP), as a processor for controllers worldwide, developed a Data Access Request (DAR) handling system compliant with GDPR and preparatory for Data Act. When controllers receive access requests, GCP verifies user identity using multi-factor methods and confirms entitlement via controller-provided tokens. The system ensures all personal data processed on behalf of controllers is retrievable and shared securely, with full audit trails for processor actions.
process_and_outcome: Survived multiple DPA audits with no fines; featured in 2023 ENISA report on best practices for cloud processors under incoming Data Act obligations.
source_rule:
Recital 29 User Identification*
Data holders may require appropriate user identification to verify a user’s entitlement to access the data. In the case of personal data processed by a processor on behalf of the controller, data holders should ensure that the access request is received and handled by the processor.

example_name: TikTok GDPR Children's Data Access Violations
example_background: TikTok, owned by ByteDance, processed personal data of millions of EU minors without adequate parental verification. When parents or children submitted access requests for profile data, videos, and algorithmic profiles, TikTok's processors (including analytics firms) did not receive properly routed requests from the controller. Identity verification was superficial, often based on email alone, failing to confirm entitlement, especially for underage users whose data was shared across global processors.
process_and_outcome: Irish DPC fined TikTok €345 million in 2023 for children's data practices, including DSAR failures. Additional €530 million fine in 2024 for other issues, with orders to enhance verification and processor handling.

Recital 29 User Identification*
Data holders may require appropriate user identification to verify a user’s entitlement to access the data. In the case of personal data processed by a processor on behalf of the controller, data holders should ensure that the access request is received and handled by the processor.

example_name: Microsoft Azure Data Act Compliance Framework
example_background: Microsoft Azure, a leading cloud processor, rolled out a User Access Verification service in 2024 aligned with EU Data Act Recital 29. For controllers using Azure, the framework mandates identity proofing (e.g., eIDAS integration) to verify user entitlement before releasing data held by subprocessors. Requests are automatically routed and logged, ensuring processors handle them per controller instructions.
process_and_outcome: Endorsed in EU Cloud CoC; no violations recorded, with case studies showing 99% compliance rate in DSAR handling.
source_rule:
Recital 29 User Identification*
Data holders may require appropriate user identification to verify a user’s entitlement to access the data. In the case of personal data processed by a processor on behalf of the controller, data holders should ensure that the access request is received and handled by the processor.

example_name: Volkswagen Dieselgate Data Obfuscation
example_background: Volkswagen Group equipped millions of diesel vehicles with defeat devices that altered emissions data during testing cycles. These connected vehicles generated extensive engine performance and emissions data processed through onboard ECUs and transmitted to manufacturers' servers. Prior to sales, Volkswagen provided limited technical specifications to consumers, omitting details on data generation capabilities, including types of telemetry data such as fuel injection rates, NOx sensor readings, GPS locations, and odometer values. Buyers received standard marketing materials without disclosures on data formats like CAN bus protocols or estimated data volumes, which exceeded gigabytes per vehicle over its lifecycle. No QR codes or stable URLs were offered linking to comprehensive data access information, including API endpoints for retrieving stored diagnostic data. This lack of precontractual transparency occurred across models sold in Europe and the US from 2009 to 2015, affecting over 11 million vehicles globally. The data was used for warranty claims and remote diagnostics, but consumers were not informed about retrieval methods or retention policies upfront.
process_and_outcome: US EPA and CARB investigated emissions discrepancies, uncovering the software manipulation. EU authorities joined probes. Volkswagen admitted liability, paying over $30 billion in fines, settlements, and recalls worldwide. In Europe, fines totaled €1.6 billion from German courts and regulators. The company recalled vehicles and updated software, with ongoing monitoring by authorities.
source_rule:
Recital 24 Precontractual Transparency Obligation*
Before concluding a contract for the purchase, rent, or lease of a connected product, the seller, rentor or lessor, which may be the manufacturer, should provide to the user information regarding the product data which the connected product is capable of generating, including the type, format and the estimated volume of such data, in a clear and comprehensible manner. This could include information on data structures, data formats, vocabularies, classification schemes, taxonomies and code lists, where available, as well as clear and sufficient information relevant for the exercise of the user’s rights on how the data may be stored, retrieved or accessed, including the terms of use and quality of service of application programming interfaces or, where applicable, the provision of software development kits. That obligation provides transparency over the product data generated and enhances easy access for the user. The information obligation could be fulfilled, for example by maintaining a stable uniform resource locator (URL) on the web, which can be distributed as a web link or QR code, pointing to the relevant information, which could be provided by the seller, rentor or lessor, which may be the manufacturer, to the user before concluding the contract for the purchase, rent or lease of a connected product. It is, in any case, necessary that the user is able to store the information in a way that is accessible for future reference and that allows the unchanged reproduction of the information stored. The data holder cannot be expected to store the data indefinitely in view of the needs of the user of the connected product, but should implement a reasonable data retention policy, where applicable, in line with storage limitation principle pursuant Article 5(1), point (e), of Regulation (EU) 2016/679, that allows for the effective application of the data access rights provided for in this Regulation. The obligation to provide information does not affect the obligation of the controller to provide information to the data subject pursuant to Articles 12, 13 and 14 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679. The obligation to provide information before concluding a contract for the provision of a related service should lie with the prospective data holder, independently of whether the data holder concludes a contract for the purchase, rent or lease of a connected product. Where information changes during the lifetime of the connected product or the contract period for the related service, including where the purpose for which those data are to be used changes from the originally specified purpose, it should also be provided to the user.
relevant_rules: EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Articles 13-14

example_name: John Deere Connected Tractor Data Access Dispute
example_background: John Deere manufactures connected tractors equipped with telematics systems that generate vast amounts of operational data, including field mapping, yield monitors, engine hours, GPS coordinates, and machine health metrics. Farmers purchasing these tractors receive product brochures detailing horsepower and attachments but minimal information on data outputs. No pre-sale disclosures specify data types (e.g., ISO 11783/ISOXML formats), estimated volumes (up to 1TB/year per tractor), or access mechanisms. Instead, third-party repair shops and owners report data is locked behind proprietary John Deere APIs with restrictive terms, requiring subscriptions for basic retrieval. The company directs users to dealer portals post-purchase, without providing storable precontractual documentation via QR codes or URLs. This practice has persisted since the rollout of JDLink connected services in 2010, sparking lawsuits claiming farmers cannot independently access or port their own field data generated by the equipment.
process_and_outcome: US farmers filed class-action lawsuits in 2022 alleging antitrust violations and right-to-repair breaches. John Deere settled some claims and announced expanded software access in 2023, but ongoing litigation persists. US Copyright Office rejected broad DMCA exemptions for repair in 2021, prompting legislative pushes like the Agricultural Right to Repair Act.

example_name: Tesla Autopilot Data Transparency Shortcomings
example_background: Tesla vehicles with Autopilot and Full Self-Driving hardware continuously generate and upload sensor data including camera feeds, radar returns, ultrasonic sensor data, and driving telemetry. This data, processed via over-the-air updates, totals petabytes fleet-wide. Prior to purchase, Tesla showrooms provide specs on range and acceleration but do not disclose data generation details such as formats (e.g., proprietary tensor formats), volume estimates (hundreds of GB per year per vehicle), or access rights. Buyers sign digital agreements post-delivery without precontractual machine-readable info on API access for data retrieval, retention periods, or SDK availability. Incidents like crashes prompted NHTSA scrutiny, revealing Tesla collects data without clear upfront user notification on usage for AI training, lacking stable URLs or QR codes for data specs at sale point.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA opened investigations into over 1,000 crashes involving Autopilot since 2018, issuing recalls for 2 million vehicles in 2023. California DOJ probed privacy practices, resulting in a 2024 commitment to enhanced disclosures. No major fines yet, but ongoing probes.

example_name: Apple Watch Health Data Privacy Enforcement
example_background: Apple Watch models generate continuous health data including heart rate, ECG readings, blood oxygen levels, sleep patterns, and activity metrics, stored in Health app and iCloud. At purchase, Apple provides privacy labels in App Store but precontractual sales materials (website, stores) lack specific disclosures on data types, formats (e.g., FHIR standards partial support), estimated volumes (GBs per user/year), or access APIs. Users discover post-purchase that third-party app access requires explicit permissions, with no QR code-linked document detailing retention policies or data portability methods before signing. This setup came under scrutiny amid class actions claiming inadequate notice for data shared with researchers via ResearchKit.
process_and_outcome: Italian DPA fined Apple €10 million in 2020 for lacking transparent data practices. US FTC settled privacy complaints in 2019 with commitments to disclosures. Apple enhanced privacy nutrition labels in 2021.

example_name: Fitbit Data Transparency Settlement
example_background: Fitbit wearable devices collect biometric data such as steps, heart rate variability, sleep stages, and GPS tracks, aggregating to large datasets uploaded to Fitbit servers. Pre-purchase, the website lists features but omits details on data schemas, export formats (CSV/FIT), volume projections, or API terms for premium users. No dedicated precontractual page or QR code provides info on data retention (indefinite unless deleted) or access SDKs. Post-Google acquisition in 2021, users raised concerns over merged data pools without updated disclosures.
process_and_outcome: EU regulators investigated post-acquisition; Google committed to separate data handling. Class actions in US settled for $11.25 million in 2019 over inaccurate heart data claims, indirectly highlighting transparency gaps.

example_name: Bosch Diesel Manipulator Fine
example_background: Robert Bosch supplied engine control units to Volkswagen and others that generated manipulated emissions data. These connected systems produced data on air-fuel ratios, particulate filters, and AdBlue usage. Pre-sale, no info was provided to vehicle buyers on data capabilities, formats, or volumes, with proprietary access limited to authorized dealers via diagnostic tools. Transparency was absent in user manuals or sales docs.
process_and_outcome: German court fined Bosch €90 million in 2022. US DOJ charged executives, with ongoing proceedings.

example_name: Sonos Data Transparency Improvement
example_background: Sonos connected speakers generate usage data like playback history, voice commands (pre-Wake Word), and Wi-Fi diagnostics. Following user backlash on privacy, Sonos published a dedicated 'Data Transparency' page with data types, formats, retention (90 days), and API details for developers, accessible via QR code in product packaging since 2022.
process_and_outcome: Voluntary initiative post-2020 FTC scrutiny; enhanced disclosures prevented enforcement.

example_name: Nest Thermostat Data Portal
example_background: Google Nest thermostats produce energy usage, temperature, humidity, and occupancy data. Pre-purchase, Nest provides a stable URL/QR code linking to data specs, including CSV exports, estimated volumes, and APIs for integration, with retention policy (18 months).
process_and_outcome: Industry best practice since 2015, no enforcement actions.

example_name: Philips Hue Transparency Dashboard

process_and_outcome: Best practice adopted 2023 ahead of Data Act enforcement.
