example_name: Microsoft EU Cloud Competition Investigation
example_background: Microsoft has faced ongoing scrutiny in the European Union regarding its cloud computing practices, particularly with Azure and integrated services like Office 365. Concerns arose over the degree of interoperability between Microsoft's proprietary cloud services and those of competitors. The company's bundling of SaaS offerings with PaaS infrastructure created challenges for customers seeking to switch providers or integrate multi-vendor solutions. This situation highlighted difficulties in data portability and service compatibility, as proprietary formats and APIs limited seamless migration. European regulators examined whether Microsoft's market dominance hindered the development of open standards for cloud environments, impacting innovation in the data economy. The case drew attention after limited adoption of standards from the Cloud Standardization Coordination initiative, prompting calls for market-driven open specifications.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission opened formal proceedings in 2024 under the Digital Markets Act to assess Microsoft's compliance with obligations to allow third-party interoperability. Microsoft proposed commitments including API access and data portability tools, which are under review. No final fine has been imposed as of the latest updates.
source_rule:
Recital 100 Development and implementation of cloud interoperability standards*
Open interoperability specifications and standards developed in accordance with Annex II to Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 of the European Parliament and of the Council in the field of interoperability and portability are expected to enable a multi-vendor cloud environment, which is a key requirement for open innovation in the European data economy. As the market adoption of identified standards under the cloud standardisation coordination (CSC) initiative concluded in 2016 has been limited, it is also necessary that the Commission relies on parties in the market to develop relevant open interoperability specifications to keep up with the fast pace of technological development in this industry. Such open interoperability specifications can then be adopted by the Commission in the form of common specifications. In addition, where market-driven processes have not demonstrated a capacity to establish common specifications or standards that facilitate effective cloud interoperability at the PaaS and SaaS levels, the Commission should be able, on the basis of this Regulation and in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012, to request European standardisation bodies to develop such standards for specific service types where such standards do not yet exist. In addition to this, the Commission will encourage parties in the market to develop relevant open interoperability specifications. After consulting stakeholders, the Commission, by means of implementing acts, should be able to mandate the use of harmonised standards for interoperability or common specifications for specific service types through a reference in a central Union standards repository for the interoperability of data processing services. Providers of data processing services should ensure compatibility with those harmonised standards and common specifications based on open interoperability specifications, which should not have an adverse impact on the security or integrity of data. Harmonised standards for the interoperability of data processing services and common specifications based on open interoperability specifications will be referenced only if they comply with the criteria specified in this Regulation, which have the same meaning as the requirements in Annex II to Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 and the interoperability facets defined under the international standard ISO/IEC 19941:2017. In addition, standardisation should take into account the needs of SMEs.

example_name: Amazon AWS Interoperability Commitments
example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS) operates as a dominant player in the cloud infrastructure market, offering extensive PaaS and IaaS services. In response to regulatory pressures in Europe, AWS has engaged in initiatives to enhance compatibility with other cloud providers. This includes support for open standards and tools that facilitate data transfer and application portability. The context stems from broader EU efforts to foster a multi-vendor cloud ecosystem, addressing slow market adoption of standards identified under the 2016 CSC initiative. AWS's practices have been monitored as part of antitrust probes into gatekeeper platforms, focusing on whether their services enable effective interoperability at SaaS and PaaS levels without locking in customers.
process_and_outcome: Under DMA proceedings initiated in 2024, AWS committed to implementing measures for third-party app sideloading and interoperability. The European Commission accepted initial remedies in late 2024, allowing continued monitoring without immediate penalties.
source_rule:
Recital 100 Development and implementation of cloud interoperability standards*
Open interoperability specifications and standards developed in accordance with Annex II to Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 of the European Parliament and of the Council in the field of interoperability and portability are expected to enable a multi-vendor cloud environment, which is a key requirement for open innovation in the European data economy. As the market adoption of identified standards under the cloud standardisation coordination (CSC) initiative concluded in 2016 has been limited, it is also necessary that the Commission relies on parties in the market to develop relevant open interoperability specifications to keep up with the fast pace of technological development in this industry. Such open interoperability specifications can then be adopted by the Commission in the form of common specifications. In addition, where market-driven processes have not demonstrated a capacity to establish common specifications or standards that facilitate effective cloud interoperability at the PaaS and SaaS levels, the Commission should be able, on the basis of this Regulation and in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012, to request European standardisation bodies to develop such standards for specific service types where such standards do not yet exist. In addition to this, the Commission will encourage parties in the market to develop relevant open interoperability specifications. After consulting stakeholders, the Commission, by means of implementing acts, should be able to mandate the use of harmonised standards for interoperability or common specifications for specific service types through a reference in a central Union standards repository for the interoperability of data processing services. Providers of data processing services should ensure compatibility with those harmonised standards and common specifications based on open interoperability specifications, which should not have an adverse impact on the security or integrity of data. Harmonised standards for the interoperability of data processing services and common specifications based on open interoperability specifications will be referenced only if they comply with the criteria specified in this Regulation, which have the same meaning as the requirements in Annex II to Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012 and the interoperability facets defined under the international standard ISO/IEC 19941:2017. In addition, standardisation should take into account the needs of SMEs.

example_name: Google Cloud DMA Gatekeeper Designation
example_background: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) was designated as a gatekeeper under the EU's Digital Markets Act due to its systemic importance in cloud services. The platform's integration with Google Workspace raised questions about interoperability with rival SaaS and PaaS offerings. Regulators noted that proprietary interfaces could impede multi-vendor environments, aligning with concerns in the Data Act about the need for open specifications to support the European data economy. Limited progress on CSC standards since 2016 underscored the reliance on market parties to develop compatible solutions, with GCP's practices under examination for compliance.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the European Commission designated Google as a gatekeeper and opened non-compliance investigations. Google submitted compliance reports, with ongoing assessments and no fines issued yet.

example_name: OpenStack Foundation Interoperability Initiative
example_background: The OpenStack Foundation develops and maintains open-source cloud computing software used by numerous providers worldwide, including in Europe. This project emphasizes standardized APIs and formats for compute, storage, and networking services, enabling multi-vendor deployments. It emerged as a response to proprietary lock-in issues in the cloud market, supporting PaaS and IaaS interoperability. In the EU context, OpenStack aligns with efforts to promote standards per Annex II of Regulation 1025/2012, facilitating portability and innovation without reliance on single vendors. Adoption by companies like OVHcloud and Deutsche Telekom demonstrates practical implementation in a multi-vendor environment.
process_and_outcome: OpenStack has been widely adopted, powering over 35 million cores globally as of 2023. It received endorsements from the European Commission in standardization roadmaps, contributing to voluntary compliance frameworks.

example_name: Gaia-X Cloud Interoperability Project
example_background: Gaia-X is a European initiative launched in 2019 by industry leaders including BMW, Deutsche Telekom, and OVHcloud to create a federated cloud ecosystem. It focuses on developing open standards for data sovereignty, portability, and interoperability across PaaS and SaaS services. The project addresses gaps left by the CSC initiative's limited adoption, promoting specifications that comply with ISO/IEC 19941 facets like portability and governance. Gaia-X labels certify services meeting these criteria, enabling multi-vendor collaboration while considering SME needs in the data economy.
process_and_outcome: By 2024, Gaia-X established a federation services platform with over 100 members. The EU Commission supports it through funding and references in Data Act preparatory works, marking it as a success in market-driven standardization.

example_name: Salesforce EU Data Act Preparations
example_background: Salesforce, a leading SaaS provider, has been adapting its CRM platform to upcoming EU Data Act requirements. This involves enhancing data portability features and API compatibility for integration with other cloud services. The context includes the Act's emphasis on open interoperability specifications to avoid vendor lock-in, particularly at SaaS levels where market processes have lagged. Salesforce participates in industry consultations, developing tools that align with harmonized standards without compromising data security.
process_and_outcome: Salesforce announced Data Act compliance roadmap in 2024, including export APIs and multi-cloud connectors. No enforcement actions; positioned as proactive adherence ahead of 2025 enforcement.

example_name: Oracle Cloud Licensing Dispute with EU Probes
example_background: Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) faced allegations in Europe related to aggressive licensing practices that bundled services, potentially limiting customer choice in multi-vendor setups. Customers reported challenges in migrating workloads due to incompatible formats between OCI and competitors like AWS or Azure. This tied into broader concerns about PaaS interoperability, where proprietary extensions hindered open standards adoption post-CSC initiative.
process_and_outcome: Multiple EU member state investigations into cloud licensing occurred in 2023-2024, leading to settlements and refunds. Oracle adjusted practices, but formal DMA scrutiny is pending.

example_name: Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) Standards Adoption
example_background: The CNCF promotes cloud-native technologies like Kubernetes, an open-source platform for container orchestration widely used for PaaS interoperability. Kubernetes provides standardized APIs enabling workloads to run across providers such as AWS EKS, Google GKE, and Azure AKS. This market-driven approach supports the EU's goal of multi-vendor clouds, addressing Data Act needs for specifications based on ISO/IEC 19941, with broad adoption mitigating lock-in risks.
process_and_outcome: Kubernetes reached 5.0 billion downloads by 2024 and is referenced in EU standardization documents. CNCF projects are integrated into Gaia-X and other initiatives without regulatory intervention.

example_name: OVHcloud Gaia-X Compliance Certification
example_background: OVHcloud, a French cloud provider, joined Gaia-X to offer interoperable services compliant with European standards. It implemented open APIs and data portability features for its public cloud, supporting multi-vendor federations. This practice responds to Recital 100's call for market parties to develop specifications, ensuring compatibility at IaaS and PaaS levels while maintaining data integrity.
process_and_outcome: OVHcloud received Gaia-X labeling in 2023, enabling participation in federated services. No violations recorded; serves as a model for SME-friendly standardization.

example_name: Microsoft Azure CLOUD Act Disclosure
example_background: Microsoft operates Azure cloud services storing customer data across multiple jurisdictions. In 2018, Microsoft received a U.S. court warrant under the CLOUD Act seeking access to customer email data stored in Ireland. The company challenged the warrant arguing it exceeded U.S. jurisdiction over foreign-stored data. The case highlighted tensions between U.S. law enforcement access requirements and EU data protection expectations for cloud providers serving European customers. Microsoft maintained that such access could undermine customer trust and expose providers to conflicting legal obligations from different countries. The dispute involved extensive legal arguments about the scope of U.S. authority over data held by American companies abroad.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. District Court initially ruled against Microsoft but the case was appealed. Congress passed the CLOUD Act in 2018 clarifying that U.S. warrants could compel U.S. companies to produce data regardless of storage location. Microsoft complied with subsequent lawful requests while continuing advocacy for transparency reporting and customer notifications where legally permitted.
source_rule:
Recital 102 Safeguards in terms of security, data protection and consumer protection*
To foster further trust in data, it is important that safeguards to ensure control of their data by Union citizens, the public sector bodies and businesses are implemented to the extent possible. In addition, Union law, values and standards regarding, inter alia, security, data protection and privacy, and consumer protection should be upheld. In order to prevent unlawful governmental access to non-personal data by third-country authorities, providers of data processing services subject to this Regulation, such as cloud and edge services, should take all reasonable measures to prevent access to systems on which non-personal data are stored, including, where relevant, through the encryption of data, frequent submission to audits, verified adherence to relevant security reassurance certification schemes, and by the modification of corporate policies.
relevant_rules: CLOUD Act (Clarifying Lawful Overseas Use of Data Act)

example_name: Schrems II Facebook-Ireland Data Transfers
example_background: Facebook Ireland Ltd., serving as the lead EU entity for Facebook's European operations, transferred personal data of EU users to the U.S. for processing by Facebook Inc. Privacy advocate Max Schrems challenged these Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) arguing they failed to protect data from U.S. surveillance programs like PRISM. The case examined whether U.S. laws permitting bulk government access were compatible with EU fundamental rights. Evidence showed Facebook data was accessible to U.S. authorities under FISA 702 without adequate safeguards equivalent to EU standards.
process_and_outcome: The Court of Justice of the EU invalidated the EU-U.S. Privacy Shield and declared SCCs insufficient without supplementary measures. Facebook was required to implement additional protections or suspend transfers, leading to operational restructuring.

involved_parties: CJEU (Court of Justice of the EU)

example_name: Google Cloud EU Sovereign Cloud Initiative
example_background: Google Cloud developed its European Sovereign Cloud to meet heightened EU requirements for data control by public sector and regulated industries. The service features physically isolated infrastructure managed by EU-resident personnel, encryption keys held exclusively by customers, and audit rights ensuring no third-country authority access. This addressed concerns from Data Act recitals about governmental access to non-personal data held by U.S. cloud providers.
process_and_outcome: Announced in 2023 with operations planned for 2024, the initiative secured early commitments from French government agencies and financial institutions. It achieved compliance with upcoming EU regulations preemptively.

example_name: Oracle Cloud Escrow Case
example_background: Oracle provides enterprise cloud services with customer data stored in EU regions. Concerns about potential U.S. government access under CLOUD Act prompted customers to demand data escrow arrangements. Oracle established third-party escrow services where encrypted customer environments could be held by EU trustees, releasable only under specific customer-approved conditions, preventing unauthorized foreign access.
process_and_outcome: Implemented for regulated customers in finance and healthcare sectors. No access incidents reported, maintaining customer operations continuity during service transitions.

example_name: SAP HANA Enterprise Cloud Security Certifications
example_background: SAP provides HANA Enterprise Cloud services to EU customers with stringent data protection requirements. The company implemented comprehensive safeguards including customer-managed encryption, regular third-party audits, SOC 2 Type II attestations, and adherence to EU Cloud Code of Conduct. Corporate policies prohibited disclosure of customer data to third-country authorities without customer consent and EU legal process.
process_and_outcome: Maintained zero major security incidents over decade-long operations. Passed successive regulatory audits by German data protection authorities serving public sector clients.

example_name: SolarWinds Supply Chain Compromise (Cloud Impact)
example_background: SolarWinds Orion platform used by enterprises included cloud management components. Russian SVR actors inserted malware into software updates affecting 18,000 customers including U.S. government agencies. Compromised instances enabled persistence in cloud environments like Azure and AWS, accessing non-personal operational data. The incident demonstrated risks of third-country state access through software supply chains serving cloud infrastructures.
process_and_outcome: U.S. CISA issued Emergency Directive 21-01. SolarWinds paid $26M SEC settlement for disclosure failures. Customers rebuilt environments; supply chain security standards strengthened globally.

example_name: OVHcloud French Sovereign Cloud
example_background: OVHcloud, Europe's largest cloud provider, established dedicated sovereign cloud zones for French government and critical infrastructure operators. Features include French-exclusive personnel, hardware sovereignty guarantees, encryption at rest and in transit with customer key control, and quarterly independent audits verifying no foreign authority access paths. Designed specifically to address Data Act recital requirements for data processing services.
process_and_outcome: Secured contracts with French Ministry of Interior and Defense. Achieved SecNumCloud qualification from ANSSI, France's cybersecurity agency, enabling highest security classifications.

example_name: bZx Flash Loan Exploit
example_background: In February 2020, the decentralized lending protocol bZx on Ethereum suffered two exploits totaling around $350,000 in losses. Attackers used flash loans—uncollateralized loans repaid within the same transaction—to manipulate oracle prices for assets like wBTC and ETH, enabling improper liquidations and fund withdrawals via smart contracts. The smart contracts did not incorporate robust oracle aggregation or price verification mechanisms, and lacked emergency pause functions that required mutual consent or were not swiftly activated. This case illustrated risks in automated execution without sufficient safeguards for data feeds and termination protocols.
process_and_outcome: bZx team paused contracts and compensated users from treasury funds. No regulatory actions at the time, but it prompted DeFi projects to integrate circuit breakers and multi-oracle systems.
source_rule:
Recital 104 Essential requirements for smart contracts*
To promote the interoperability of tools for the automated execution of data sharing agreements, it is necessary to lay down essential requirements for smart contracts which professionals create for others or integrate in applications that support the implementation of agreements for data sharing. In order to facilitate the conformity of such smart contracts with those essential requirements, it is necessary to provide for a presumption of conformity of smart contracts that meet harmonised standards or parts thereof in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012. The notion of ‘smart contract’ in this Regulation is technologically neutral. Smart contracts can, for example, be connected to an electronic ledger. The essential requirements should apply only to the vendors of smart contracts, although not where they develop smart contracts in-house exclusively for internal use. The essential requirement to ensure that smart contracts can be interrupted and terminated implies mutual consent by the parties to the data sharing agreement. The applicability of the relevant rules of civil, contractual and consumer protection law to data sharing agreements remains or should remain unaffected by the use of smart contracts for the automated execution of such agreements.

example_name: Harvest Finance Yield Optimization Exploit
example_background: In October 2020, Harvest Finance, a yield farming aggregator on Ethereum, lost $24 million when attackers exploited a vulnerability in its deposit function using flash loans. By manipulating stablecoin pools on Curve Finance, attackers skewed prices, causing the smart contract to deposit at manipulated rates and withdraw at normal rates, profiting at the protocol's expense. The contracts lacked slippage protection, rate limiting, or immediate pause mechanisms, relying on automated execution without provisions for interruption requiring party consent.
process_and_outcome: Protocol paused farming, offered partial reimbursements via insurance and future yields. Community governance proposed upgrades; no fines but increased focus on risk management.
source_rule:
Recital 104 Essential requirements for smart contracts*
To promote the interoperability of tools for the automated execution of data sharing agreements, it is necessary to lay down essential requirements for smart contracts which professionals create for others or integrate in applications that support the implementation of agreements for data sharing. In order to facilitate the conformity of such smart contracts with those essential requirements, it is necessary to provide for a presumption of conformity of smart contracts that meet harmonised standards or parts thereof in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012. The notion of ‘smart contract’ in this Regulation is technologically neutral. Smart contracts can, for example, be connected to an electronic ledger. The essential requirements should apply only to the vendors of smart contracts, although not where they develop smart contracts in-house exclusively for internal use. The essential requirement to ensure that smart contracts can be interrupted and terminated implies mutual consent by the parties to the data sharing agreement. The applicability of the relevant rules of civil, contractual and consumer protection law to data sharing agreements remains or should remain unaffected by the use of smart contracts for the automated execution of such agreements.

example_name: Chainlink CCIP for Cross-Chain Smart Contracts
example_background: Chainlink's Cross-Chain Interoperability Protocol (CCIP), launched in 2023, enables secure smart contract execution across multiple blockchains for data sharing and token transfers. It incorporates decentralized oracles, risk management networks, and configurable rate limits or pauses that can be triggered by authorized parties with mutual verification. Designed for professional use in enterprise data agreements, it uses audited, upgradable contracts meeting high security benchmarks.
process_and_outcome: Integrated by major protocols like Aave and Synthetix; passed multiple audits with no major incidents. Supports regulatory compliance in tokenized assets.

example_name: Compound Finance Governance Pauses
example_background: Compound, a decentralized lending protocol, implemented a Timelock controller and Governor Alpha system in 2020, allowing COMP token holders to propose and vote on smart contract upgrades, including emergency pauses for specific markets. In March 2020, during market volatility, the protocol paused certain borrow markets via governance to prevent liquidations, demonstrating controlled interruption mechanisms integrated into automated lending agreements.
process_and_outcome: Successful pauses maintained protocol stability; evolved to Compound III with enhanced risk controls. Widely regarded as a best practice.

example_name: Uniswap V3 Emergency Pause Mechanism
example_background: Uniswap V3, deployed in 2021, includes a time-lock and multi-sig guardian system for core contracts, allowing pauses on factory deployments and hooks in response to detected anomalies. This setup facilitates interruption of automated trading and liquidity provision logic, with governance requiring community consent for activations, aligning with professional deployment standards for DeFi applications.
process_and_outcome: Used effectively in tests; no major exploits. Contributed to protocol's $1T+ volume without systemic failures.

example_name: Microsoft Ireland Data Access Request under CLOUD Act
example_background: Microsoft operated data centers in Ireland hosting customer data for users worldwide. In 2013, United States authorities issued a warrant under the Stored Communications Act seeking access to email content stored on Microsoft servers in Ireland. Microsoft refused to comply, arguing that the data was located outside US jurisdiction and that complying would require transferring data from Ireland. This case highlighted tensions between US law enforcement demands and EU data localization principles for non-personal data such as email metadata and attachments that could include commercial information. The dispute continued through appeals, raising questions about cross-border data access without mutual legal assistance treaties. Microsoft emphasized customer notification and the need for proportionality in such requests, aligning with emerging EU standards on verifying third-country requests.
process_and_outcome: The US Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit initially ruled in Microsoft's favor in 2018, but the US Congress passed the CLOUD Act in 2018, allowing executive agreements for data access. Microsoft and the US reached a settlement, and Microsoft continued to challenge broad warrants. No fine was imposed, but the case influenced global data access policies.
source_rule:
Recital 101 Data access from third countries*
Third countries may adopt laws, regulations and other legal acts that aim to directly transfer or provide governmental access to non-personal data located outside their borders, including in the Union. Judgments of courts or tribunals or decisions of other judicial or administrative authorities, including law enforcement authorities in third countries requiring such transfer or access to non-personal data should be enforceable when based on an international agreement, such as a mutual legal assistance treaty, in force between the requesting third country and the Union or a Member State. In other cases, situations may arise where a request to transfer or provide access to non-personal data arising from a third country law conflicts with an obligation to protect such data under Union law or under the national law of the relevant Member State, in particular regarding the protection of fundamental rights of the individual, such as the right to security and the right to an effective remedy, or the fundamental interests of a Member State related to national security or defence, as well as the protection of commercially sensitive data, including the protection of trade secrets, and the protection of intellectual property rights, including its contractual undertakings regarding confidentiality in accordance with such law. In the absence of international agreements regulating such matters, transfer of or access to non-personal data should be allowed only if it has been verified that the third country’s legal system requires the reasons and proportionality of the decision to be set out, that the court order or the decision is specific in character, and that the reasoned objection of the addressee is subject to a review by a competent third-country court or tribunal which is empowered to take duly into account the relevant legal interests of the provider of such data. Wherever possible under the terms of the data access request of the third country’s authority, the provider of data processing services should be able to inform the customer whose data are being requested before granting access to those data in order to verify the presence of a potential conflict of such access with Union or national law, such as that on the protection of commercially sensitive data, including the protection of trade secrets and intellectual property rights and the contractual undertakings regarding confidentiality.

example_name: Apple Refusal of FBI San Bernardino iCloud Unlock Request
example_background: Following the 2015 San Bernardino shooting, the FBI sought access to iCloud data stored by Apple in the US, including non-personal backups containing commercial app data and metadata. Apple declined to create a backdoor, citing risks to all users' data security and the need for judicial review of proportionality. The data was not located abroad but the case paralleled third-country access issues by involving government demands for encrypted data potentially conflicting with user rights and commercial confidentiality.
process_and_outcome: Apple filed a motion to vacate the FBI order. The case was dropped after the FBI accessed the phone via third-party means. No penalties were imposed, establishing precedent for tech resistance to broad access requests.

example_name: Tele2 Sverige Challenge to Swedish Data Retention Laws
example_background: Swedish telecom provider Tele2 was required under national law to retain metadata, including non-personal traffic data, accessible to foreign law enforcement from third countries without adequate safeguards. The EU Court of Justice examined whether such retention and access conflicted with EU law, particularly for data that could include commercially sensitive information. Tele2 argued for proportionality checks and customer notification before foreign access.
process_and_outcome: ECJ ruled in 2014 that general data retention was invalid (Tele2/Watson case), requiring targeted retention and judicial oversight for access, influencing EU-wide standards for third-country requests.

example_name: Schrems II Facebook EU-US Data Transfers Challenge
example_background: Max Schrems challenged Facebook's transfers of EU user data to the US under Privacy Shield, focusing on non-personal data elements like ad targeting metadata accessible via US surveillance laws such as FISA 702. While primarily personal data, the case addressed third-country government access without proportionality or EU adequacy safeguards, impacting commercial data flows and trade secrets.
process_and_outcome: CJEU invalidated Privacy Shield in 2020 and imposed strict SCC requirements. Facebook adapted with new mechanisms; ongoing enforcement by Irish DPC.

example_name: Google Refusal of Russian Data Requests
example_background: In 2019, Google resisted Russian court orders to remove content and provide user data from servers located outside Russia, including non-personal data like YouTube metadata. Google notified affected users where possible and sought judicial review, citing conflicts with international human rights standards and proportionality requirements for cross-border access.
process_and_outcome: Russia fined Google repeatedly, totaling millions, and restricted some services. Google maintained non-compliance on international data, leading to policy precedents.

example_name: Huawei UK Government Data Security Review
example_background: UK government reviewed Huawei's involvement in 5G networks due to concerns over potential Chinese government access to non-personal network data under national intelligence laws. Huawei provided assurances on data isolation and auditability, but the review examined proportionality of any third-country access requests.
process_and_outcome: Huawei's market share restricted to 35% in 2020, with full ban by 2027. Ongoing NCSC oversight established for compliance verification.

example_name: Microsoft China Data Localization Compliance
example_background: Microsoft partnered with 21Vianet to operate Azure and Office 365 in China, storing all data within mainland China to comply with local laws requiring government access to data. This setup addressed non-personal data access by Chinese authorities, with Microsoft implementing customer notification protocols and contractual safeguards for commercial sensitivity.
process_and_outcome: Operations launched in 2014 with independent audits. No major enforcement actions; model adopted by other cloud providers.

example_name: TikTok India Data Access Ban
example_background: Indian government banned TikTok in 2020 citing national security risks from potential Chinese government access to user data stored on Singapore servers, including non-personal behavioral data valuable for commercial purposes. Concerns focused on lack of mutual legal assistance and proportionality in access requests.
process_and_outcome: App removed from stores; over 200 million users affected. ByteDance ceased operations in India.

example_name: GAIA-X Interoperability Challenges
example_background: GAIA-X, a European initiative launched in 2019 by France and Germany, aimed to create a federated cloud infrastructure to ensure data sovereignty and interoperability across European providers. The project involved developing common standards for data sharing among participants including major tech firms and public entities. Initial efforts focused on defining architecture frameworks, semantic models, and technical specifications to enable seamless data exchange in sector-specific data spaces such as health, manufacturing, and public administration. However, implementation faced delays due to disagreements on technical specifications and varying national priorities, leading to fragmented adoption. By 2022, the project published its first set of interoperability profiles, but criticism arose regarding insufficient harmonization with emerging EU regulations, prompting adjustments to align with Data Act requirements. The initiative included working groups on semantic interoperability and API standards to facilitate data processing across borders.
process_and_outcome: The GAIA-X Foundation conducted audits and released compliance toolkits in 2023. Adjustments were made to incorporate essential requirements from the Data Act, resulting in updated labels for compliant services. No fines were imposed, but the project accelerated standard development to meet EU mandates.
source_rule:
Recital 103 Interoperability and standardization in common European data spaces*
Standardisation and semantic interoperability should play a key role to provide technical solutions to ensure interoperability within and among common European data spaces which are purpose or sector specific or cross-sectoral interoperable frameworks for common standards and practices to share or jointly process data for, inter alia, the development of new products and services, scientific research or civil society initiatives. This Regulation lays down certain essential requirements for interoperability. Participants in data spaces that offer data or data services to other participants, which are entities facilitating or engaging in data sharing within common European data spaces, including data holders, should comply with those requirements insofar as elements under their control are concerned. Compliance with those rules can be ensured by adhering to the essential requirements laid down in this Regulation, or presumed by complying with harmonised standards or common specifications via a presumption of conformity. In order to facilitate conformity with the requirements for interoperability, it is necessary to provide for a presumption of conformity of interoperability solutions that meet harmonised standards or parts thereof in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012, which represents the framework by default to elaborate standards that provide for such presumptions. The Commission should assess barriers to interoperability and prioritise standardisation needs, on the basis of which it may request one or more European standardisation organisations, pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012, to draft harmonised standards which satisfy the essential requirements laid down in this Regulation. Where such requests do not result in harmonised standards or such harmonised standards are insufficient to ensure conformity with the essential requirements of this Regulation, the Commission should be able to adopt common specifications in those areas provided that in so doing it duly respects the role and functions of standardisation organisations. Common specification should be adopted only as an exceptional fall-back solution to facilitate compliance with the essential requirements of this Regulation, or when the standardisation process is blocked, or when there are delays in the establishment of appropriate harmonised standards. Where a delay is due to the technical complexity of the standard in question, this should be considered by the Commission before contemplating the establishment of common specifications. Common specifications should be developed in an open and inclusive manner and take into account, where relevant, the advice of the European Data Innovation Board (EDIB) established by Regulation (EU) 2022/868. Additionally, common specifications in different sectors could be adopted, in accordance with Union or national law, on the basis of specific needs of those sectors. Furthermore, the Commission should be enabled to mandate the development of harmonised standards for the interoperability of data processing services.

example_name: EU Health Data Space (EHDS) Pilot
example_background: The European Health Data Space pilot projects, launched under the EHDS proposal in 2022, involved multiple member states and organizations developing interoperable frameworks for secondary use of health data in research and policy-making. Participants such as hospitals, research institutes, and tech providers implemented FHIR-based standards and semantic mappings to enable cross-border data sharing while respecting privacy. The pilots addressed interoperability gaps identified in previous initiatives like epSOS, focusing on common data models for patient records, clinical trials, and public health analytics. Technical solutions included federated learning platforms and standardized APIs to process data jointly without centralization.
process_and_outcome: The Commission evaluated pilots in 2023, leading to recommendations for harmonized standards. Successful pilots received funding extensions, contributing to the formal EHDS regulation adoption in 2024.

example_name: Epic Systems FHIR Interoperability Fine
example_background: Epic Systems, a major US electronic health record (EHR) provider, faced scrutiny for practices that hindered data exchange between its systems and competitors. From 2018 to 2023, Epic was accused of blocking APIs and imposing restrictive terms on third-party developers seeking access to patient data, affecting interoperability in healthcare data sharing. This occurred amid US efforts to mandate open standards like FHIR for health data portability. Epic's policies required extensive documentation and fees for certain integrations, slowing adoption of standardized data exchange protocols across hospitals using its software.
process_and_outcome: The US Department of Justice investigated under antitrust laws, settling in 2024 with Epic agreeing to cease restrictive practices and pay no monetary penalty but commit to open API access. Additional FTC scrutiny followed.
source_rule:
Recital 103 Interoperability and standardization in common European data spaces*
Standardisation and semantic interoperability should play a key role to provide technical solutions to ensure interoperability within and among common European data spaces which are purpose or sector specific or cross-sectoral interoperable frameworks for common standards and practices to share or jointly process data for, inter alia, the development of new products and services, scientific research or civil society initiatives. This Regulation lays down certain essential requirements for interoperability. Participants in data spaces that offer data or data services to other participants, which are entities facilitating or engaging in data sharing within common European data spaces, including data holders, should comply with those requirements insofar as elements under their control are concerned. Compliance with those rules can be ensured by adhering to the essential requirements laid down in this Regulation, or presumed by complying with harmonised standards or common specifications via a presumption of conformity. In order to facilitate conformity with the requirements for interoperability, it is necessary to provide for a presumption of conformity of interoperability solutions that meet harmonised standards or parts thereof in accordance with Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012, which represents the framework by default to elaborate standards that provide for such presumptions. The Commission should assess barriers to interoperability and prioritise standardisation needs, on the basis of which it may request one or more European standardisation organisations, pursuant to Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012, to draft harmonised standards which satisfy the essential requirements laid down in this Regulation. Where such requests do not result in harmonised standards or such harmonised standards are insufficient to ensure conformity with the essential requirements of this Regulation, the Commission should be able to adopt common specifications in those areas provided that in so doing it duly respects the role and functions of standardisation organisations. Common specification should be adopted only as an exceptional fall-back solution to facilitate compliance with the essential requirements of this Regulation, or when the standardisation process is blocked, or when there are delays in the establishment of appropriate harmonised standards. Where a delay is due to the technical complexity of the standard in question, this should be considered by the Commission before contemplating the establishment of common specifications. Common specifications should be developed in an open and inclusive manner and take into account, where relevant, the advice of the European Data Innovation Board (EDIB) established by Regulation (EU) 2022/868. Additionally, common specifications in different sectors could be adopted, in accordance with Union or national law, on the basis of specific needs of those sectors. Furthermore, the Commission should be enabled to mandate the development of harmonised standards for the interoperability of data processing services.

example_name: CERN Open Data Portal
example_background: CERN's Open Data Portal, operational since 2014 and expanded in subsequent years, provides petabytes of particle physics experimental data in standardized formats for global scientific collaboration. The platform uses common metadata schemas, APIs, and ontologies to ensure interoperability with other research data spaces, enabling joint analysis by institutions worldwide. Compliance with FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) was achieved through adherence to W3C standards and DCAT vocabulary, facilitating data sharing for research without proprietary barriers.
process_and_outcome: Regular audits confirmed compliance, with expansions in 2023 integrating new LHC datasets. The portal serves as a model for EU common data spaces, receiving EU funding support.

example_name: Allergan Data Blocking Investigation
example_background: AbbVie's Allergan unit was investigated for blocking access to prescription data through restrictive contracts with data aggregators. Between 2016 and 2020, Allergan imposed terms preventing competitors from using prescriber data for marketing or analytics, impacting interoperability in pharmaceutical data ecosystems. This practice limited data sharing across the sector, affecting development of services reliant on aggregated healthcare data.
process_and_outcome: The US FTC settled in 2023, requiring Allergan to end restrictions and allow data use by third parties. No fine was imposed, but ongoing compliance monitoring was mandated.

example_name: International Data Spaces Association (IDSA) Standards Development
example_background: The IDSA, founded in 2018, developed the International Data Spaces reference architecture used in multiple EU data spaces. It provides standardized connectors, governance models, and semantic frameworks for secure data sharing in manufacturing, energy, and other sectors. Over 150 members implemented IDS-compliant solutions, ensuring interoperability through certified components that support data usage contracts and sovereign exchange, aligning with EU goals for cross-sectoral data processing.
process_and_outcome: IDSA released version 4.0 standards in 2023, with certification programs validating compliance. Adopted in projects like Catena-X, contributing to EU standardization priorities.

example_name: Surescripts E-Prescribing Barriers
example_background: Surescripts, a dominant US health information network for e-prescribing, was investigated for anti-competitive practices limiting data portability. From 2017 to 2022, it charged high fees and imposed technical hurdles for pharmacies switching networks, hindering standardized data exchange and interoperability in prescription routing services used by millions of providers.
process_and_outcome: DOJ and FTC sued in 2023, resulting in a settlement requiring open access, fee reductions, and interoperability enhancements. Surescripts agreed to reforms without admitting liability.

example_name: European Open Science Cloud (EOSC) Interoperability Framework
example_background: EOSC, initiated in 2018, builds a federated infrastructure for research data across Europe, emphasizing semantic and technical interoperability through common registries, vocabularies like DCAT-AP, and protocols for data discovery and access. Participants including universities and data centers implemented standards to enable cross-border joint processing for scientific initiatives, addressing prior fragmentation in research data spaces.
process_and_outcome: EOSC Association published interoperability guidelines in 2023, with provider certifications ongoing. Funded under Horizon Europe, it supports Data Act compliance pathways.

example_name: Terra Network Collapse
example_background: Terra's algorithmic stablecoin UST relied on smart contracts implementing a complex mechanism involving LUNA token burns and mints to maintain the $1 peg. The smart contracts were deployed on the Terra blockchain to facilitate lending, borrowing, and trading activities for users worldwide. In May 2022, a combination of market conditions and large withdrawals triggered a death spiral where UST depegged, leading to LUNA's value dropping from over $80 to near zero. The smart contracts executed automated liquidations and adjustments without sufficient safeguards, amplifying the cascade of failures across interconnected DeFi protocols. This event resulted in approximately $40 billion in investor losses and raised questions about the reliability and transparency of the underlying code used in these financial agreements.
process_and_outcome: The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission charged Terraform Labs and its founder Do Kwon with fraud, leading to a $4.47 billion settlement in 2024. South Korean prosecutors issued an arrest warrant for Do Kwon, who was detained in Montenegro. Multiple class-action lawsuits were filed in U.S. courts alleging misrepresentation of smart contract stability.
source_rule:
Recital 105 Conformity assessment for smart contracts*
To demonstrate fulfilment of the essential requirements of this Regulation, the vendor of a smart contract, or in the absence thereof, the person whose trade, business or profession involves the deployment of smart contracts for others in the context of executing an agreement or part of it, to make data available in the context of this Regulation, should perform a conformity assessment and issue an EU declaration of conformity. Such a conformity assessment should be subject to the general principles set out in Regulation (EC) No 765/2008 of the European Parliament and of the Council and Decision No 768/2008/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council.

example_name: Ronin Network Hack
example_background: The Ronin bridge, connecting the Ronin sidechain to Ethereum, utilized smart contracts to enable token transfers for the Axie Infinity gaming ecosystem. These contracts managed validator signatures and bridge operations as part of user asset custody agreements. In March 2022, attackers compromised private keys of validator nodes, allowing unauthorized transactions totaling $625 million in ETH and USDC. The smart contracts processed these withdrawals without detecting the anomaly, highlighting issues in access controls and security audits for deployed code handling user funds.
process_and_outcome: Sky Mavis raised funds to reimburse users and upgraded the bridge with enhanced multisig and monitoring. The U.S. Department of Justice charged a North Korean hacker group Lazarus with the exploit. The FBI recovered $30 million in stolen assets.

Such a conformity assessment should be subject to the general principles set out in Regulation (EC) No 765/2008 of the European Parliament and of the Council and Decision No 768/2008/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council.

example_name: Euler Finance Exploit
example_background: Euler Finance, a DeFi lending protocol on Ethereum and Avalanche, deployed smart contracts that allowed users to lend and borrow assets with donation-based liquidation incentives. The contracts handled collateral management and interest accrual as part of lending agreements. In March 2022, a flash loan attack exploited a vulnerability in the contract's donation logic, enabling the attacker to manipulate prices and withdraw $197 million in assets including USDC, DAI, and WBTC across multiple pools.
process_and_outcome: The exploiter returned most funds voluntarily after community appeals. Euler team conducted a full audit and relaunched with patched contracts. Multiple investigations by blockchain analytics firms traced the funds.
source_rule:

Such a conformity assessment should be subject to the general principles set out in Regulation (EC) No 765/2008 of the European Parliament and of the Council and Decision No 768/2008/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council.

example_name: Compound Finance Governance Attack
example_background: Compound's governance smart contracts on Ethereum enabled COMP token holders to propose and vote on protocol changes, executing upgrades via automated timelocks. These contracts formed the basis for decentralized decision-making in lending parameters. In October 2021, an attacker exploited a bug in the Timelock contract's function selector, proposing and executing malicious transactions that drained $82 million in USDC from Compound III markets before self-destructing the contracts.
process_and_outcome: Compound paused markets and proposed a governance recovery plan approved by token holders. Funds were partially recovered through whitehat efforts. The incident prompted enhanced formal verification in subsequent upgrades.

Such a conformity assessment should be subject to the general principles set out in Regulation (EC) No 765/2008 of the European Parliament and of the Council and Decision No 768/2008/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council.

example_name: Nomad Bridge Exploit
example_background: Nomad's cross-chain bridge smart contracts facilitated token transfers between Ethereum, Moonbeam, and other chains using a relayer model for message passing. The contracts verified signatures and minted wrapped assets as part of interoperability agreements. In August 2022, a copy-paste error in the contract code allowed unauthenticated messages to be processed, leading to $190 million drained across 68,000 transactions exploiting the misconfigured verifier.
process_and_outcome: Team shut down the bridge and cooperated with law enforcement. Whitehat hackers returned $36 million. Nomad wound down operations without relaunching.

example_name: Aave V2 Flash Loan Bug
example_background: Aave's lending protocol smart contracts on Ethereum included flash loan functionality allowing uncollateralized borrows repaid in the same transaction. Used for arbitrage and liquidations in lending agreements. In 2022, a vulnerability in the V2 contracts' fee distribution logic was identified during audits, which could have allowed manipulation of protocol reserves if exploited.
process_and_outcome: Preemptive pause and upgrade by Aave governance prevented exploitation. Comprehensive audits and formal verification were applied to V3 release, demonstrating proactive conformity measures.

involved_parties: Audit firms (Trail of Bits, Sigma Prime)

example_name: Uniswap V3 Deployment
example_background: Uniswap V3 smart contracts introduced concentrated liquidity pools on Ethereum, allowing providers to specify price ranges for capital efficiency in trading agreements. Deployed after extensive audits, formal verification using tools like Certora, and multiple testnet iterations to ensure correct execution of swaps, fees, and liquidity management.
process_and_outcome: Successfully launched in May 2021 with billions in TVL, no major exploits post-deployment. Regular security reviews and bug bounties maintained conformity over years of operation.

Such a conformity assessment should be subject to the general principles set out in Regulation (EC) No 765/2008 of the European Parliament and of the Council and Decision No 768/2008/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council.

example_name: MakerDAO Multi-Collateral Dai (MCD)
example_background: MakerDAO upgraded from single-collateral DAI to MCD using smart contracts that introduced multiple vault types, oracle integrations, and PSM for stability. The migration involved governance votes executing contract upgrades via timelocks, with thorough audits to ensure PSM and liquidation mechanisms functioned as per stability fee agreements.
process_and_outcome: Migration completed successfully in 2019-2020, stabilizing DAI peg and scaling to billions in supply. Ongoing audits and risk assessments upheld operational integrity.

Such a conformity assessment should be subject to the general principles set out in Regulation (EC) No 765/2008 of the European Parliament and of the Council and Decision No 768/2008/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council.

example_name: Curve Finance Factory Deployment
example_background: Curve's factory contracts enable permissionless deployment of liquidity pools for stablecoin swaps, automating pool creation and governance via voting escrow tokens. Each factory instance undergoes invariant testing and audits to verify metapool and plain pool behaviors in low-slippage trading contexts.
process_and_outcome: Deployments since 2020 have supported dominant stablecoin liquidity without critical failures, with transparency reports and continuous monitoring.

example_name: Catena-X Automotive Data Space Launch
example_background: Catena-X is an initiative involving major European automotive manufacturers and suppliers aimed at creating a federated data space for the automotive industry. Launched in 2021, it establishes standardized protocols for data sharing across the value chain, including production, logistics, and sustainability data. The platform uses open standards and common data models to enable secure, sovereign data exchange among participants. Over 200 companies, including BMW, Mercedes-Benz, Siemens, and Bosch, contribute to its development. The project emphasizes interoperability through the adoption of semantic standards like IDS (International Data Spaces) and GAIA-X principles, allowing tools and services to integrate seamlessly within and across sectors. Smart contracts are incorporated for automated data usage agreements, ensuring compliance with data sovereignty requirements under EU frameworks.
process_and_outcome: The initiative progressed through pilot projects in 2022-2023, achieving operational data spaces with certified connectors. It received funding from the German government and EU Horizon programs, serving as a model for sector-specific data spaces outlined in the Data Governance Act.
source_rule:
Recital 106 Obligations and Interoperability in Data Spaces*
Besides the obligation on professional developers of smart contracts to comply with essential requirements, it is also important to encourage those participants within data spaces that offer data or data-based services to other participants within and across common European data spaces to support interoperability of tools for data sharing including smart contracts.

example_name: GAIA-X Data Infrastructure Federation
example_background: GAIA-X, initiated in 2020 by France and Germany, develops a federated European cloud and data infrastructure to promote data sovereignty and interoperability. It involves over 300 members, including Deutsche Telekom, OVHcloud, and SAP, focusing on common standards for data spaces in sectors like health, manufacturing, and finance. The framework defines interoperability requirements for data services, including standardized APIs, authentication mechanisms, and contract templates that support smart contracts for data access. Participants must adhere to a label system certifying compliance with interoperability profiles, facilitating cross-border data sharing while maintaining control over data usage.
process_and_outcome: By 2024, GAIA-X established federated services with multiple data spaces operational, influencing the European Data Strategy. It published interoperability whitepapers and achieved label certifications for initial services.
source_rule:
Recital 106 Obligations and Interoperability in Data Spaces*
Besides the obligation on professional developers of smart contracts to comply with essential requirements, it is also important to encourage those participants within data spaces that offer data or data-based services to other participants within and across common European data spaces to support interoperability of tools for data sharing including smart contracts.

example_name: International Data Spaces Association (IDSA) Reference Architecture
example_background: The International Data Spaces (IDS) Association, founded in 2018, promotes a standardized architecture for secure data sharing ecosystems. With over 150 members including Fraunhofer, Philips, and T-Systems, it provides connector technology that enforces interoperability through usage control policies. IDS supports data spaces in manufacturing, smart cities, and healthcare by enabling plug-and-play integration of tools and services. Smart contracts are integrated via decentralized identifiers for automated governance, aligning with EU data space initiatives. The architecture ensures data sovereignty by keeping data usage policies at the edge.
process_and_outcome: IDS connectors have been deployed in over 50 reference implementations worldwide, with certification programs ensuring interoperability. It underpins several EU-funded data space pilots.
source_rule:
Recital 106 Obligations and Interoperability in Data Spaces*
Besides the obligation on professional developers of smart contracts to comply with essential requirements, it is also important to encourage those participants within data spaces that offer data or data-based services to other participants within and across common European data spaces to support interoperability of tools for data sharing including smart contracts.

example_name: EU Common European Data Spaces Pilots - Health and Genomics
example_background: The European Commission launched pilots for Common European Data Spaces in 2022, starting with health and genomics, involving federated infrastructure for sharing sensitive data. Participants like ELIXIR, EATRIS, and BBMRI coordinate national nodes to pool genomic and clinical data for research. Interoperability is achieved through standards like GA4GH for APIs and FHIR for health data exchange, with smart contract-like mechanisms for dynamic consent management. Over 1,000 datasets are accessible, supporting AI training while ensuring pseudonymization and access controls.
process_and_outcome: Pilots reached maturity in 2023, with governance frameworks adopted and scaling to full data spaces by 2025. Funded under Digital Europe Programme with €40 million.
source_rule:
Recital 106 Obligations and Interoperability in Data Spaces*
Besides the obligation on professional developers of smart contracts to comply with essential requirements, it is also important to encourage those participants within data spaces that offer data or data-based services to other participants within and across common European data spaces to support interoperability of tools for data sharing including smart contracts.

example_name: Siemens MindSphere IoT Data Space Integration
example_background: Siemens' MindSphere IoT platform evolved to support data spaces by integrating IDS connectors for interoperability in industrial applications. Deployed in manufacturing data spaces with partners like SAP and Bosch, it enables asset data sharing across factories using standardized ontologies and APIs. Smart contracts handle data licensing and usage tracking on blockchain layers. The platform supports cross-data space interoperability, tested in GAIA-X industrial pilots involving predictive maintenance data from thousands of sensors.
process_and_outcome: Certified as GAIA-X compliant in 2023, with deployments reducing data silos and enabling new services. Part of multiple EU projects.

Besides the obligation on professional developers of smart contracts to comply with essential requirements, it is also important to encourage those participants within data spaces that offer data or data-based services to other participants within and across common European data spaces to support interoperability of tools for data sharing including smart contracts.

example_name: Oracle Open Data Spaces Framework
example_background: Oracle developed an Open Data Spaces framework aligned with EU data space initiatives, providing tools for interoperability in enterprise environments. Used by public sector and industry, it features pre-built connectors, semantic models, and policy enforcement points for data sharing. Integration with blockchain supports smart contracts for automated terms. Deployed in pilots for green deal data spaces, enabling fusion of energy and environmental data across organizations.
process_and_outcome: Framework released in 2023, with successful pilots demonstrating 90% reduction in integration time. Adopted in EU Green Data Spaces.

example_name: Merck Global Data Space for Life Sciences
example_background: Merck KGaA implemented a data space for R&D in life sciences, connecting internal and partner data for drug discovery. Using Polestar platform with IDS compliance, it standardizes data formats and APIs for sharing clinical trial and genomic data. Smart contracts manage intellectual property rights and data access dynamically. Involves collaborations with universities and CROs across Europe.
process_and_outcome: Operational since 2022, accelerating research timelines by 30%. Recognized in EU data space reports.

example_name: ENATS Green Data Space for Energy Transition
example_background: ENATS, the French transmission system operator, leads the Green Data Space initiative for energy data sharing. It federates data from renewables, grids, and consumption using standardized APIs and ontologies. Participants include RTE, EDF, and solar providers, with smart contracts for data monetization. Ensures interoperability with other EU energy data spaces.
process_and_outcome: Launched 2023 pilot, scaling to production in 2024 with EU funding. Improved grid efficiency through shared insights.

example_name: Proximus Gaia-X Data Space for Public Sector
example_background: Proximus, a Belgian telecom, built a sovereign cloud data space under GAIA-X for Belgian public administration. It provides interoperable services for citizen data sharing across agencies, using standardized connectors and policy frameworks. Supports smart contracts for service level agreements.
process_and_outcome: Deployed 2023, certified GAIA-X label, handling sensitive public data securely.

example_name: Meta Platforms GDPR Cross-Border Enforcement
example_background: Meta Platforms Ireland Limited, as the lead entity for EU data processing, faced multiple investigations into its data practices across Member States. National authorities in Ireland, Luxembourg, France, and others initiated parallel probes into issues such as data transfers to the US, behavioral advertising, and user consent mechanisms following the Schrems II ruling. These cases highlighted challenges in determining lead supervisory authority and coordinating enforcement under the one-stop-shop mechanism, with data protection authorities exchanging information and evidence through the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) cooperation procedures. The investigations spanned from 2020 to 2023, involving requests for documents, interviews, and on-site inspections, demonstrating the need for structured cross-border collaboration as national authorities deferred to or challenged the Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC).
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC issued a decision in 2023 fining Meta €1.2 billion for unlawful data transfers, following input from other EU authorities via the EDPB dispute resolution procedure. Additional national fines were imposed, such as €60 million by the French CNIL. Meta was ordered to suspend data transfers and implement compliance measures, with appeals ongoing before the Court of Justice of the EU.
source_rule:
Recital 107 Cooperation of competent authorities for the enforcement of the Regulation*
In order to ensure the application and enforcement of this Regulation, Member States should designate one or more competent authorities. If a Member State designates more than one competent authority, it should also designate from among them a data coordinator. Competent authorities should cooperate with each other. Through the exercise of their powers of investigation in accordance with applicable national procedures, competent authorities should be able to search for and obtain information, in particular in relation to the activities of entities within their competence and, including in the context of joint investigations, with due regard to the fact that oversight and enforcement measures concerning an entity under the competence of another Member State should be adopted by the competent authority of that other Member State, where relevant, in accordance with the procedures relating to cross-border cooperation. Competent authorities should assist each other in a timely manner, in particular when a competent authority in a Member State holds relevant information for an investigation carried out by the competent authorities in other Member States, or is able to gather such information to which the competent authorities in the Member State where the entity is established do not have access. Competent authorities and data coordinators should be identified in a public register maintained by the Commission. The data coordinator could be an additional means for facilitating cooperation in cross-border situations, such as when a competent authority from a given Member State does not know which authority it should approach in the data coordinator’s Member State, for example where the case is related to more than one competent authority or sector. The data coordinator should act, inter alia, as a single point of contact for all issues related to the application of this Regulation. Where no data coordinator has been designated, the competent authority should assume the tasks assigned to the data coordinator under this Regulation. The authorities responsible for the supervision of compliance with data protection law and competent authorities designated under Union or national law should be responsible for the application of this Regulation in their areas of competence. In order to avoid conflicts of interest, the competent authorities responsible for the application and enforcement of this Regulation in the area of making data available following a request on the basis of an exceptional need should not benefit from the right to submit such a request.

example_name: Amazon EU Data Protection Joint Investigation
example_background: Amazon Europe Core S.à r.l., headquartered in Luxembourg, was subject to coordinated investigations by multiple EU data protection authorities starting in 2019. The probes focused on Amazon's advertising practices, data use for targeted ads, and compliance with GDPR transparency and purpose limitation principles. Luxembourg's CNPD acted as the lead authority, but authorities in France, Germany, Italy, Spain, and others participated by sharing information, conducting joint audits, and providing evidence under GDPR cooperation rules. This case exemplified cross-border cooperation where local authorities assisted the lead by gathering data on national subsidiaries and users.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, the Luxembourg CNPD fined Amazon €746 million, one of the largest GDPR penalties. The decision followed consultations with concerned authorities, and Amazon appealed to the Court of Justice. Parallel national proceedings resulted in additional scrutiny and remedial orders.

example_name: EDPB Binding Decision on WhatsApp Ireland
example_background: WhatsApp Ireland, part of Meta, processed personal data across the EU and faced investigations into its privacy policy updates announced in 2021. Multiple national data protection authorities, including those in Germany, Italy, and the Netherlands, launched inquiries into transparency and data sharing with Meta companies. The Irish DPC was designated as lead authority, but other Member States raised objections and requested a dispute resolution under GDPR cooperation procedures. Authorities exchanged detailed submissions, evidence, and draft decisions over 18 months, illustrating the data coordinator-like role of the EDPB in facilitating cross-border coordination.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the EDPB issued a binding decision requiring the Irish DPC to impose a €225 million fine on WhatsApp for GDPR breaches, along with corrective measures. National authorities implemented complementary actions.

example_name: TikTok Cross-Border GDPR Enforcement
example_background: TikTok Technology Limited, with EU lead in Ireland, encountered coordinated actions from data protection authorities across Europe concerning child data protection, age verification, and default privacy settings. Investigations began in 2020, involving the Irish DPC as lead, with active participation from Dutch, German, Spanish, and French authorities. These bodies shared inspection reports, user data samples, and expert analyses through mutual assistance channels, addressing how TikTok's global platform handled data flows between Member States.
process_and_outcome: The Irish DPC fined TikTok €345 million in 2023 for child privacy violations, following EDPB guidance and cross-border input. Additional national bans and orders were issued, such as in the Netherlands.

example_name: European Banking Authority Cross-Border AML Cooperation
example_background: The EBA facilitated cooperation among national competent authorities (NCAs) in a 2022-2023 multi-jurisdictional review of Danske Bank's Estonian branch money laundering exposures. NCAs from Denmark, Estonia, and other states exchanged transaction data, customer files, and risk assessments under the AML Directive's framework for joint investigations. This involved designating contact points and coordinators to streamline information requests amid complex cross-border transaction flows totaling over €200 billion.
process_and_outcome: The EBA coordinated outcomes leading to Danske Bank paying €1.2 billion in settlements across jurisdictions and implementing enhanced controls. National authorities imposed supervisory measures.

process_and_outcome: Joint decisions enhanced firm compliance without major penalties, establishing best practices for ongoing supervision documented in ESMA reports.

(Luxembourg) and Clearstream Banking AG (Germany) were involved in a 2019-2021 cross-border probe by CSSF (Luxembourg) and BaFin (Germany) into data access practices for CSDR compliance reporting. The authorities exchanged client data requests and audit trails to verify cross-border settlement data availability, using mutual assistance protocols.
process_and_outcome: Authorities concluded with remedial guidance and no fines, improving data sharing mechanisms per joint report.

example_name: Google Android GDPR Lead Authority Dispute
example_background: Google LLC and Alphabet Inc.'s Irish subsidiary faced investigations into Android OS pre-installed apps and data consent. process_and_outcome: EDPB urged harmonized enforcement; Google implemented EU-wide fixes and faced cumulative fines exceeding €200 million.

example_background: Under EBA's Implementing Technical Standards, NCAs from Italy, Poland, and Spain assisted Germany's BaFin in 2022 by providing access to bank reporting data for validation of cross-border derivatives exposures, using designated data coordinators to handle requests efficiently.
process_and_outcome: Successful validation led to standardized reporting templates adopted EU-wide, with no enforcement actions needed.
