involved_parties: US ONC (Office of the National Coordinator for Health IT)

example_name: Google Android Fine
example_background: Google implemented agreements with device manufacturers and carriers requiring the pre-installation of Google apps such as the Play Store, Chrome browser, and Search as default applications on Android devices. These agreements included revenue-sharing arrangements and restrictions on the development or distribution of competing search or browser apps. This conduct covered over 90% of global Android devices and was designed to maintain Google's dominance in general search services and Android OS distribution. The European Commission conducted an investigation starting in 2015 after complaints from rivals like FairSearch and 31 national competition authorities.
process_and_outcome: In July 2018, the European Commission imposed a fine of €4.34 billion on Google for abusing its dominant position. Google appealed the decision, and in September 2022, the General Court largely upheld the fine, reducing it slightly to €4.125 billion. Google further appealed to the European Court of Justice.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Relation to Art. 101 and 102 TFEU*
This Regulation should not affect the application of the rules of competition, in particular Articles 101 and 102 TFEU. The measures provided for in this Regulation should not be used to restrict competition in a manner contrary to the TFEU.

example_name: Google Shopping Fine
example_background: Google gave preferential placement to its own comparison shopping service in its universal search results while demoting rival comparison shopping services. This self-preferencing occurred regardless of relevance or user preferences from 2008 to 2016, impacting competitors' traffic and revenues. The investigation began in 2010 following a complaint from comparison shopping service Foundem.
process_and_outcome: In June 2017, the European Commission fined Google €2.42 billion. Google appealed, and the fine was upheld by the General Court in November 2021. Google lodged a further appeal.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Relation to Art. 101 and 102 TFEU*
This Regulation should not affect the application of the rules of competition, in particular Articles 101 and 102 TFEU. The measures provided for in this Regulation should not be used to restrict competition in a manner contrary to the TFEU.

example_name: Intel CPU Rebates Fine
example_background: From October 2002 to December 2006, Intel engaged in loyalty rebates and naked rebates to computer manufacturers and distributors worldwide. These payments conditioned on the manufacturers buying all or most of their x86 CPUs from Intel, effectively foreclosing competitors like AMD from the market. Intel's conduct resulted in it having over 70-80% market share in x86 CPUs.
process_and_outcome: In May 2009, the European Commission imposed a record fine of €1.06 billion on Intel. The fine was upheld through multiple appeals, including by the General Court in 2022, confirming the abusive nature of the rebates.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Relation to Art. 101 and 102 TFEU*
This Regulation should not affect the application of the rules of competition, in particular Articles 101 and 102 TFEU. The measures provided for in this Regulation should not be used to restrict competition in a manner contrary to the TFEU.

example_name: Microsoft Windows Media Player Fine
example_background: Microsoft tied its Windows Media Player to the Windows PC operating system, refusing to license Windows without the player bundled. This bundling limited competition in the media player market, where competitors like RealNetworks and Apple struggled to gain distribution. The case was part of broader scrutiny of Microsoft's dominance in operating systems.
process_and_outcome: In March 2004, the European Commission fined Microsoft €497 million and required it to offer a version of Windows without Media Player. Further fines of €899 million followed for non-compliance in 2008.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Relation to Art. 101 and 102 TFEU*
This Regulation should not affect the application of the rules of competition, in particular Articles 101 and 102 TFEU. The measures provided for in this Regulation should not be used to restrict competition in a manner contrary to the TFEU.

example_name: Qualcomm Chipset Abuse Fine
example_background: Qualcomm imposed exclusivity rebates on Apple from 2011 to 2016, paying Apple billions to ensure it bought substantially all modem chips from Qualcomm and not from competitors like MediaTek or Intel. This foreclosed rivals from the premium smartphone chipset market, where Qualcomm held around 75% share.
process_and_outcome: In January 2018, the European Commission fined Qualcomm €997 million. The fine was upheld by the General Court in June 2022.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Relation to Art. 101 and 102 TFEU*
This Regulation should not affect the application of the rules of competition, in particular Articles 101 and 102 TFEU. The measures provided for in this Regulation should not be used to restrict competition in a manner contrary to the TFEU.

example_name: Amazon Prime Bundling Investigation
example_background: Amazon was investigated for self-preferencing its own products in search results and bundling Prime Video with other services, potentially restricting competition in online marketplaces and video streaming. The probe examined whether these practices leveraged Amazon's dominance to disadvantage third-party sellers and rival services.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the European Commission opened a formal investigation under Article 102 TFEU, ongoing as of 2024 with no final decision yet.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Relation to Art. 101 and 102 TFEU*
This Regulation should not affect the application of the rules of competition, in particular Articles 101 and 102 TFEU. The measures provided for in this Regulation should not be used to restrict competition in a manner contrary to the TFEU.

example_name: Apple App Store Fees Probe
example_background: Apple was examined for imposing high commission fees (30%) on app developers via the App Store, its sole distribution channel for iOS apps, and restricting developers from informing users about alternative payment options. This potentially exploited Apple's control over the iOS ecosystem.
process_and_outcome: In March 2024, the European Commission charged Apple with breaching the Digital Markets Act, which complements TFEU competition rules, with ongoing proceedings.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Relation to Art. 101 and 102 TFEU*
This Regulation should not affect the application of the rules of competition, in particular Articles 101 and 102 TFEU. The measures provided for in this Regulation should not be used to restrict competition in a manner contrary to the TFEU.

example_name: Booking.com Online Travel Agency Fine
example_background: Booking.com used most-favoured-nation clauses in contracts with hotels, preventing them from offering lower rates or better conditions on competing platforms. This parity clause stifled price competition in online hotel booking from 2015 to 2019.
process_and_outcome: In December 2023, France's competition authority fined Booking.com €413.4 million for anti-competitive clauses.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Relation to Art. 101 and 102 TFEU*
This Regulation should not affect the application of the rules of competition, in particular Articles 101 and 102 TFEU. The measures provided for in this Regulation should not be used to restrict competition in a manner contrary to the TFEU.
involved_parties: French Competition Authority (Autorité de la concurrence)

example_name: Microsoft Compliance with Browser Choice
example_background: Following the 2009 settlement of its browser tying case, Microsoft implemented a Browser Choice screen for Windows users in Europe, allowing selection of non-IE browsers. This remedied the prior bundling and restored competition in the browser market.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft complied with Commission monitoring until 2014, with periodic reviews confirming effective implementation and increased browser competition.
source_rule:
Recital 116 Relation to Art. 101 and 102 TFEU*
This Regulation should not affect the application of the rules of competition, in particular Articles 101 and 102 TFEU. The measures provided for in this Regulation should not be used to restrict competition in a manner contrary to the TFEU.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on Data Act Draft
example_background: The European Commission prepared the draft Regulation on harmonised rules on fair access to and use of data (Data Act) as part of its data strategy to boost Europe's data economy. The proposal aimed to facilitate data sharing between users and manufacturers of connected devices, establish rules for switching between cloud and edge service providers, and enable public sector access to non-personal data held by private entities during emergencies. To ensure alignment with data protection standards, consultation with supervisory authorities was required under the EU data protection framework for EU institutions. The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) and the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) reviewed the draft, focusing on aspects such as the definition of data, obligations for data holders, lawful access conditions, and safeguards for fundamental rights.
process_and_outcome: On 4 May 2022, the EDPS and EDPB jointly delivered Opinion 06/2022 providing recommendations on data protection compliance, including clarifications on personal data processing, lawful bases for access, and reinforcement of GDPR principles. The Commission incorporated several suggestions in subsequent versions of the regulation.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Consultation of the EDPO and EDPB*
The European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board were consulted in accordance with Article 42(1) and (2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and delivered their opinion on 4 May 2022.

example_name: EDPS Opinion on Eurodac Regulation Recast
example_background: The European Commission proposed a recast of the Eurodac Regulation to expand its scope for improved migration management, including new categories of fingerprints and facial images for comparison against asylum and visa databases. The proposal involved processing of biometric and other personal data across EU Member States. Given the impact on data protection, formal consultation with the EDPS was mandated to assess compliance with EU data protection rules applicable to Union institutions.
process_and_outcome: The EDPS provided Opinion 4/2020 on 17 June 2020, highlighting issues with data minimization, purpose limitation, and storage periods, and suggesting amendments. The opinion influenced the final trilogue negotiations, leading to adjustments in the adopted regulation.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Consultation of the EDPO and EDPB*
The European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board were consulted in accordance with Article 42(1) and (2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and delivered their opinion on 4 May 2022.

example_name: EDPB and EDPS Joint Opinion on European Health Data Space
example_background: The Commission proposed the Regulation on the European Health Data Space (EHDS) to facilitate secondary use of electronic health data for research, policy-making, and innovation while ensuring high protection standards. The initiative covered both personal health data and non-personal data, requiring assessment of interfaces with GDPR and EU institutional data protection rules.
process_and_outcome: In December 2022, the EDPS and EDPB issued a joint opinion identifying gaps in safeguards for secondary use, consent mechanisms, and rights of data subjects, with recommendations adopted in parts during legislative process.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Consultation of the EDPO and EDPB*
The European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board were consulted in accordance with Article 42(1) and (2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and delivered their opinion on 4 May 2022.

example_name: EDPS Consultation on Digital Services Act
example_background: The Digital Services Act (DSA) proposal addressed intermediary services, systemic risks, and transparency obligations for online platforms operating in the EU, involving extensive processing of user data. As an EU institution legislative measure, it necessitated consultation with data protection authorities to align with fundamental rights.
process_and_outcome: EDPS issued Opinion 6/2020 on 23 November 2020, recommending stronger data protection clauses, risk assessments for fundamental rights, and oversight mechanisms. Several recommendations were integrated into the final DSA.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Consultation of the EDPO and EDPB*
The European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board were consulted in accordance with Article 42(1) and (2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and delivered their opinion on 4 May 2022.

example_name: EDPS Opinion on AI Act Proposal
example_background: The Artificial Intelligence Act aimed to regulate AI systems based on risk levels, including provisions for data processing in high-risk applications like biometric identification and critical infrastructure. The proposal's data-intensive nature required input from data protection experts.
process_and_outcome: EDPS provided Opinion 10/2020 on 29 October 2020, focusing on data governance, transparency, and human oversight. The opinion contributed to refinements in the AI Act's data protection chapters during trilogues.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Consultation of the EDPO and EDPB*
The European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board were consulted in accordance with Article 42(1) and (2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and delivered their opinion on 4 May 2022.

example_name: EDPB Opinion on Data Governance Act
example_background: The Data Governance Act (DGA) sought to create a framework for data intermediation services, European data spaces, and public sector data reuse, emphasizing voluntary data sharing with protections for personal data involved in altruism-based reuse.
process_and_outcome: EDPB delivered Formal Opinion 03/2021 on 12 May 2021, advising on GDPR interoperability, lawful processing bases, and safeguards, influencing the final regulation's data protection provisions.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Consultation of the EDPO and EDPB*
The European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board were consulted in accordance with Article 42(1) and (2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and delivered their opinion on 4 May 2022.

example_name: EDPS Consultation on Digital Markets Act
example_background: The Digital Markets Act (DMA) targeted gatekeeper platforms with obligations on data access, interoperability, and self-preferencing, directly impacting data handling practices of large online services.
process_and_outcome: In Opinion 3/2020 dated 20 October 2020, EDPS recommended enhancements to data portability, contestability, and fairness principles aligned with data protection law, with elements reflected in the adopted text.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Consultation of the EDPO and EDPB*
The European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board were consulted in accordance with Article 42(1) and (2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and delivered their opinion on 4 May 2022.

example_name: EDPS and EDPB Opinions on Data Act Development
example_background: Prior to the formal recital 118 consultation, preliminary views were sought on the Data Act's data access mechanisms for IoT devices, cloud switching, and public emergency access, involving potential personal data flows.
process_and_outcome: Separate preliminary opinions from EDPS (2021) and EDPB discussions fed into the joint opinion of 4 May 2022, ensuring balanced data sharing rules with protection guarantees in the final Data Act.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Consultation of the EDPO and EDPB*
The European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board were consulted in accordance with Article 42(1) and (2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and delivered their opinion on 4 May 2022.

example_name: EDPS Opinion on ePrivacy Regulation Proposal
example_background: The proposed ePrivacy Regulation updated rules on privacy in electronic communications, covering metadata, end-to-end encryption, and tracking technologies, requiring alignment with GDPR via supervisory consultation.
process_and_outcome: EDPS issued multiple opinions (e.g., 5/2016, 10/2017) critiquing consent exemptions and storage periods, shaping ongoing legislative debates despite delays in adoption.
source_rule:
Recital 118 Consultation of the EDPO and EDPB*
The European Data Protection Supervisor and the European Data Protection Board were consulted in accordance with Article 42(1) and (2) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725 and delivered their opinion on 4 May 2022.

example_name: GDPR Preparation by GDPR.eu Campaign
example_background: In anticipation of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) applying from May 25, 2018, the EU launched the GDPR.eu awareness campaign and various organizations across member states undertook extensive preparations. Companies conducted data mapping exercises, updated privacy policies, implemented consent management platforms, appointed Data Protection Officers where required, and invested in employee training programs. For instance, large enterprises like those in the tech sector revised data processing agreements with vendors to ensure compliance with new rules on data transfers, accountability, and rights like data portability. This collective effort involved reviewing millions of data flows and technical systems to align with the regulation's requirements before the applicability date.
process_and_outcome: The EU Commission reported widespread readiness, with minimal disruptions upon enforcement start. Many organizations self-certified compliance through frameworks like ISO 27701, avoiding initial enforcement waves that targeted non-compliant entities.
source_rule:
Recital 117 Date of Applicability of the Regulation*
In order to allow actors within the scope of this Regulation to adapt to the new rules provided for herein, and to make the necessary technical arrangements, those rules should apply from 12 September 2025.

example_name: CNIL Google GDPR Fine
example_background: In 2019, France's CNIL data protection authority assessed Google's implementation of GDPR compliance shortly after its applicability. The investigation found that Google's privacy policy was too general, failing to provide sufficient transparency about data processing purposes, especially for personalized advertising based on extensive user profiling across services like Search, YouTube, and Android. Consent mechanisms did not meet legal requirements for being freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous, with pre-ticked boxes and bundled consents.
process_and_outcome: CNIL imposed a €50 million fine on Google in January 2019, the first major GDPR penalty. Google appealed but lost subsequent court challenges.
source_rule:
Recital 117 Date of Applicability of the Regulation*
In order to allow actors within the scope of this Regulation to adapt to the new rules provided for herein, and to make the necessary technical arrangements, those rules should apply from 12 September 2025.
involved_parties: CNIL (Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés)

example_name: Apple Privacy Compliance Initiatives
example_background: Apple has integrated privacy-by-design principles into its ecosystem, particularly emphasizing on-device processing for features like Siri, Face ID, and iCloud Private Relay. Ahead of various data regulations, Apple developed tools such as App Tracking Transparency (ATT), requiring explicit user consent for cross-app tracking, and invested in differential privacy for aggregate data analytics. These measures ensure minimal data collection and transfer, aligning with upcoming requirements for data access and portability by storing health and fitness data locally with optional encrypted cloud sync.
process_and_outcome: Apple avoided major regulatory fines and received praise from privacy advocates. ATT led to industry-wide adoption, reducing unauthorized tracking.
source_rule:
Recital 117 Date of Applicability of the Regulation*
In order to allow actors within the scope of this Regulation to adapt to the new rules provided for herein, and to make the necessary technical arrangements, those rules should apply from 12 September 2025.

example_name: PSD2 Compliance by European Banks
example_background: Under the Revised Payment Services Directive (PSD2), effective from 2018 with strong customer authentication (SCA) mandated from 2019, major European banks like ING and BBVA prepared by developing open banking APIs. They established technical infrastructures for secure data sharing with third-party providers (TPPs), implemented sandbox environments for testing, and conducted customer communication campaigns to explain new consent flows for account information services. This preparation ensured smooth transition to real-time data access and payment initiation services upon regulatory applicability.
process_and_outcome: National competent authorities granted authorizations with minimal issues. Banks reported successful TPP integrations, fostering competition without significant disruptions.
source_rule:
Recital 117 Date of Applicability of the Regulation*
In order to allow actors within the scope of this Regulation to adapt to the new rules provided for herein, and to make the necessary technical arrangements, those rules should apply from 12 September 2025.

example_name: Apple App Store Practices Investigation
example_background: Apple's App Store policies required developers to use Apple's in-app purchase system for digital goods, charging a 30% commission, and prohibited alternative payment methods or sideloading apps on iOS devices in the EEA. This model controlled access to a large customer base and data from app usage, raising questions about fairness in revenue sharing and access to end-user data for third-party developers. The practices were examined in the context of the growing app economy and cross-border digital services.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission opened formal proceedings in 2021 under the Digital Markets Act preparatory phase. In March 2024, Apple was designated a gatekeeper and ordered to allow third-party app stores and payment systems, with ongoing compliance monitoring. Separate DMA fines were issued for non-compliance in other areas.
source_rule:
Recital 119 Compatibility of the regulation with Art. 5 TEU*
Since the objectives of this Regulation, namely ensuring fairness in the allocation of value from data among actors in the data economy and fostering fair access to and use of data in order to contribute to establishing a genuine internal market for data, cannot be sufficiently achieved by the Member States but can rather, by reason of the scale or effects of the action and cross-border use of data, be better achieved at Union level, the Union may adopt measures, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity as set out in Article 5 of the Treaty on European Union. In accordance with the principle of proportionality as set out in that Article, this Regulation does not go beyond what is necessary in order to achieve those objectives,

example_name: Amazon e-Commerce Data Use Fine
example_background: Amazon was found to use data obtained from independent sellers on its marketplace to develop competing products, giving it an advantage in product recommendations and pricing. This practice involved non-public business data from third parties active on the platform, affecting competition in online retail across multiple EU member states. The marketplace's scale enabled collection of vast amounts of seller and consumer data, influencing market dynamics.
process_and_outcome: In July 2024, the European Commission fined Amazon €1.128 billion under competition rules. The decision requires Amazon to cease using marketplace seller data for competitive purposes, with appeals expected.

example_name: Spotify and Music Streaming Label Deals Investigation
example_background: Major music labels entered into agreements with streaming services that included most-favored-nation clauses, potentially restricting labels from offering better terms or data access to new entrants. This affected the EU digital music market, where streaming platforms rely on licensing deals for content and associated user data, impacting market entry for smaller players.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission conducted an antitrust probe starting in 2019, closing it in 2021 without fines after labels committed to avoid restrictive clauses for five years. Streaming services like Spotify cooperated in providing evidence.

example_name: Meta Data Access Practices DMA Fine
example_background: Meta's platforms, Facebook and Instagram, were designated as gatekeepers under the DMA. They were found to combine personal data across services without free user choice, using it for targeted advertising, which limited data portability and access for competing ad services. This occurred in the context of cross-border data flows in social media.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the European Commission issued a €797 million fine for breaches, requiring changes to data combination practices and ongoing audits.

example_name: Microsoft Azure Cloud Commitments
example_background: Microsoft offered interoperability commitments for its Azure cloud services, allowing customers and competitors greater access to data and features. This addressed prior concerns about bundling Office 365 with Azure, promoting fairer data sharing in the cloud computing market across Europe.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the European Commission accepted Microsoft's DMA compliance measures, including zero warranties for Windows Server/Azure migrations and data portability tools, avoiding fines.

example_name: Booking.com Gatekeeper Commitments
example_background: Booking.com, as a DMA gatekeeper, implemented measures to allow hotels to link directly to their own websites for bookings without parity clauses that previously restricted direct offers. This improved access to customer data and bookings for hotels operating cross-border.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission approved Booking.com's compliance plan in 2024, with monitoring to ensure fair access and no equivalent alternatives clauses.

example_name: Qualcomm Chipset Licensing Abuse Fine
example_background: Qualcomm engaged in predatory pricing for chipsets and imposed exclusivity on Apple, limiting competition in baseband chip markets. This affected data transmission technologies used in mobile devices across the EU, where modem data handling is critical.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission fined Qualcomm €997 million in 2018 for abuse of dominance. The General Court upheld the decision in 2022.

example_name: Alphabet (Google) Search Shopping Fine
example_background: Google favored its own comparison shopping service in search results, demoting competitors, which impacted data-driven advertising and shopping services in the EU internal market.
process_and_outcome: Fined €2.42 billion in 2017 by the European Commission; upheld by General Court in 2021, under appeal at CJEU.
